translation: Alif, Lam, Ra. These are the verses of the wise Book
commentary:
الٓر ۚ تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ

Alif Lam Ra. Those are the miraculous signs of the book possessing wisdom—the wise book.

Notice that Alif Lam Ra is not a separate ayah here. These are the disjointed letters that Allah ﷻ has chosen to place in His book, and you cannot contain them within a logical, grammatical or linguistic construct. This is Allah's ﷻ word, and He has complete authority over it. This is from the unseen. Allah ﷻ decides when these letters constitute an ayah by themselves and when they don't, and our intellect is put in check as we try to understand them.

But here's what's beautiful: Allah ﷻ says go ahead and try to figure it out if you want, but you should know that whether you can understand these letters or not, the book is full of wisdom. The phrase الْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ tells us that whether we grasp every mystery or not, there is profound wisdom embedded in this revelation.

translation: Have the people been amazed that We revealed [revelation] to a man from among them, [saying], "Warn mankind and give good tidings to those who believe that they will have a [firm] precedence of honor with their Lord"? [But] the disbelievers say, "Indeed, this is an obvious magician
commentary:
أَكَانَ لِلنَّاسِ عَجَبًا أَنْ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ رَجُلٍ مِّنْهُمْ أَنْ أَنذِرِ ٱلنَّاسَ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَنَّ لَهُمْ قَدَمَ صِدْقٍ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ ۗ قَالَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَسَـٰحِرٌ مُّبِينٌ

Has it been something strange for people that We have sent revelation to a man from among them? For those studying grammar, note the construction أَنْ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ رَجُلٍ مِّنْهُمْ—this is the khabar (predicate) of kana.

But what exactly was Allah ﷻ commanding this messenger to do? أَنْ أَنذِرِ ٱلنَّاسَ—warn the people. وَبَشِّرِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟—and give good news to those who believe. Is that what they find so strange? Notice something critical here: warnings are mentioned first, then good news. You'll find that the entire tone of this Surah emphasizes warning over congratulations. The theme of caution overshadows comfort throughout.

Allah ﷻ tells the believers they will have قَدَمَ صِدْقٍ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ—a firm footing of truth with their Master. In other words, they will have precedence, priority, the first step as far as their Lord is concerned.

But look at how the disbelievers responded: قَالَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَسَـٰحِرٌ مُّبِينٌ—"This is truly an obvious magician." The more they listened to the Prophet ﷺ, the more convinced they became that he was performing some kind of sorcery.

Here's something fascinating: calling him a magician is actually a compliment, not an insult. Why? Because when you call something magic, you're acknowledging that you have to take a leap of faith to believe it's just a trick. You're admitting it's something awesome that you cannot explain through common sense. They were acknowledging the supernatural power of the Quran when they called it magic.

But think about what they were calling magic. The Prophet ﷺ wasn't levitating in the air. He wasn't showing them elaborate tricks. There was no staff turning into a snake here, no sea parting. None of that was happening. All he had was words, and they were calling words magic. Usually when you think of magic, you think of something visual, something you'd see—not words. But these words were mystifying them to such a degree that they couldn't find any other category to put them in except magic.

Even in their rejection of faith, there's an implicit acknowledgment that something from the unseen is at work, because magic itself is a belief in the unseen. So even in these accusations, there's a subtle defeat of their argument.

translation: Indeed, your Lord is Allah, who created the heavens and the earth in six days and then established Himself above the Throne, arranging the matter [of His creation]. There is no intercessor except after His permission. That is Allah, your Lord, so worship Him. Then will you not remember
commentary:
إِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ فِى سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ ۖ يُدَبِّرُ ٱلْأَمْرَ ۖ مَا مِن شَفِيعٍ إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ إِذْنِهِۦ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ ۚ أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ

Now Allah ﷻ reminds them of something fundamental: How can you reject this messenger when revelation has come to him? By rejecting him, you're forgetting that he has a Master—and that Master is your Master too.

Your Master is Allah ﷻ, the One who created the skies and the earth فِى سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ—in six days. Let me pause here on this word أَيَّامٍ, which is the plural of يَوْمٍ, meaning day. Our count of days is based on the rotation of the Earth—the rising and setting of the sun. That's how we calculate a 24-hour day. But when Allah ﷻ is creating the universe, there is no sun and moon yet. Those objects aren't even there. So the count for what constitutes a "day" in this context cannot possibly mean what we think of as a day. It's something else entirely, something far more grand.

Sometimes Allah ﷻ wants to give us some sense of perspective, so He provides proportions in the Quran. He'll say that some days with Him are equivalent to this much of your time, and other days are equivalent to that much. It's almost like the relativity of time—the proportions aren't always consistent. A day with Allah ﷻ is not the same as our day, and even those proportions vary. This is a double layer of wonder.

ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ—then He rose over the Throne. We don't know precisely what this means, and we're not going to venture into speculation about it. What we do know is what comes next.

يُدَبِّرُ ٱلْأَمْرَ—He plans in detail. The word تَدْبِير refers to long-term, meticulous planning. It's calculated, well-thought-out planning. Allah ﷻ makes decisions—not just one decision, but many decisions. Yet He describes all His decisions as though they were just one unified decision, so perfect and comprehensive is His plan.

Then comes a critical statement: مَا مِن شَفِيعٍ إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ إِذْنِهِۦ—there will not be anyone to make intercession except after His permission. Don't think you can ridicule this Book and undermine this Messenger because some intercessor will come and protect you later. There is no intercession except after Allah's ﷻ permission, and that permission itself is part of His master plan.

The Quraysh had this notion that they would have intercessors on the Day of Judgment—idols, ancestors, someone who would speak on their behalf. An intercessor's job is to present new information: "You're ready to fire him, but let me tell you, he's actually really good and you don't know the whole story because you weren't there." That's how intercession works in worldly courts—the defense attorney brings up mitigating factors, new evidence that changes the judgment.

But can you do that with Allah ﷻ? Can you present Him with information He doesn't already know? Can you say, "New evidence has emerged that You should consider"? Of course not. He already knows everything that could possibly be considered. So the entire framework of intercession operates solely under His permission and His knowledge.

ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ—That is Allah ﷻ, your Master, so enslave yourselves to Him. أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ—Why don't you make even a little effort to remember?

translation: To Him is your return all together. [It is] the promise of Allah [which is] truth. Indeed, He begins the [process of] creation and then repeats it that He may reward those who have believed and done righteous deeds, in justice. But those who disbelieved will have a drink of scalding water and a painful punishment for what they used to deny
commentary:
إِلَيْهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيعًا ۖ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقًّا ۚ إِنَّهُۥ يَبْدَؤُا۟ ٱلْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُۥ لِيَجْزِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَهُمْ شَرَابٌ مِّنْ حَمِيمٍ وَعَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْفُرُونَ

إِلَيْهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيعًا—To Him alone, all of you are going to return. This directly addresses their misconception. They thought that on Judgment Day, they would first go to their intercessors, who would then deal with Allah ﷻ on their behalf, and everything would work out. Christians would look for Isa (AS) on that Day. Those who worshipped graves—even among Muslims who made this terrible mistake with righteous scholars—would look for people like Abdul Qadir Jilani (may Allah ﷻ have mercy on him), not understanding that these great scholars don't even know what's being done at their graves and what claims are being made in their names.

But Allah ﷻ makes it crystal clear: you are returning to Him alone. Nobody else. You're not reporting to anybody else first.

وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقًّا—This is the promise of Allah ﷻ, and it is true. إِنَّهُۥ يَبْدَؤُا۟ ٱلْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُۥ—He is the One who creates the first time, and then He will make it happen again. Why are you so doubtful about resurrection?

Think about it logically: What's harder, doing something the first time or the second time? When you do something for the first time, it's difficult, it's new, there's uncertainty. But when you have to do it again, you already have the experience—it's easy, you've got this. So why would you doubt that Allah ﷻ can create a second time? If anything, the first creation should be enough evidence that He can definitely do it again.

لِيَجْزِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ بِٱلْقِسْطِ—So that He can compensate those who believed and did the good deeds expected of them, with justice. He will do it openly, with complete fairness.

وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَهُمْ شَرَابٌ مِّنْ حَمِيمٍ—And those who disbelieved, they will have a drink from hameem. حَمِيم in Arabic comes from the root related to heating up or rising temperature. Intense body temperature or fever is also called hama. Let me describe hameem for you—and I can speak from experience because this has happened to me.

Imagine you're on a road trip in the summer. There's no rest stop anywhere. You have a water bottle in the car that you left there, and it's been sitting under the sun all day. The water inside has become intensely hot. You're dying of thirst, so you open it and say Bismillah, and that really intensely hot water not only burns, it has a terrible smell to it. That's hameem—water that is burning hot because of prolonged exposure to extreme heat, and it carries a foul odor with it.

That's what they'll be given to drink. وَعَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْفُرُونَ—And they will have painful torment because of the disbelief they continuously engaged in.

translation: It is He who made the sun a shining light and the moon a derived light and determined for it phases - that you may know the number of years and account [of time]. Allah has not created this except in truth. He details the signs for a people who know
commentary:
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ ٱلشَّمْسَ ضِيَآءً وَٱلْقَمَرَ نُورًا وَقَدَّرَهُۥ مَنَازِلَ لِتَعْلَمُوا۟ عَدَدَ ٱلسِّنِينَ وَٱلْحِسَابَ ۚ مَا خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ ذَٰلِكَ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ يُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ

Speaking of hameem—water made burning hot by exposure to the sun—Allah ﷻ now draws our attention to that very sun.

هُوَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ ٱلشَّمْسَ ضِيَآءً—He is the One who placed the sun as a source of hot, bright light. The word ضِيَآء refers to light that has heat in it, light that burns. وَٱلْقَمَرَ نُورًا—And He made the moon a source of noor, light that doesn't have any heating or burning quality to it.

Notice the precision: diya is mentioned with the sun, noor is mentioned with the moon. Two different types of light for two different purposes.

وَقَدَّرَهُۥ مَنَازِلَ—And He calculated them in different stages. He made the sun and the moon traverse through specific phases and positions. لِتَعْلَمُوا۟ عَدَدَ ٱلسِّنِينَ وَٱلْحِسَابَ—so that you may come to know the count of years and keep your calculations.

All our calendars—whether solar or lunar—are based on what Allah ﷻ does with the sun and the moon. These heavenly bodies are our timekeepers.

مَا خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ ذَٰلِكَ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ—Allah ﷻ didn't create any of that except with very precise purpose and reason. Now here's the deeper implication: If Allah ﷻ created the sun and moon with purpose—so we can keep track of time—then we must also have a purpose. If these celestial bodies serve as timers for us, then we probably have a task to accomplish in the limited time we have on this Earth. The timer itself is evidence that there's something we're supposed to be doing.

يُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ—He thoroughly explains these ayat for a nation that wants to know. Notice the verb here: يَعْلَمُونَ suggests people who want to know. The desire to know is key, because there are people who are so relaxed in this world, so comfortable, that they don't even want to know. They have no interest in seeking these answers.

translation: Indeed, in the alternation of the night and the day and [in] what Allah has created in the heavens and the earth are signs for a people who fear Allah
commentary:
إِنَّ فِى ٱخْتِلَـٰفِ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ وَمَا خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَتَّقُونَ

إِنَّ فِى ٱخْتِلَـٰفِ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ—Certainly in the alternation of night and day. Night and day are mentioned here because the previous ayah just discussed the sun and moon—there's a natural connection to this constant battle and exchange between darkness and light.

وَمَا خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ—And whatever Allah ﷻ created in the skies and the earth. لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَتَّقُونَ—There truly are miraculous signs for people who want to protect themselves, who seek to guard themselves.

This is important. The previous ayah mentioned قَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ—people who want to know. Now it's قَوْمٍ يَتَّقُونَ—people who want to protect themselves through taqwa. This tells us something critical: wanting to know by itself doesn't suffice. Curiosity alone is not enough.

I've met plenty of intellectuals who study Islam as a fascinating culture. They've read the Quran multiple times and find it quite remarkable, truly remarkable actually. Their curiosity is satisfied, but they didn't come to the Quran because they want protection for their lives. They don't see themselves in any kind of danger, spiritual or otherwise. So the Quran becomes entertaining reading for them, intellectually stimulating perhaps, but nothing more than that.

But the Quran is not merely for those who want to know—it's for those who recognize they need protection, who understand they are in spiritual danger without guidance. That's the difference between intellectual curiosity and genuine taqwa.

translation: Indeed, those who do not expect the meeting with Us and are satisfied with the life of this world and feel secure therein and those who are heedless of Our signs
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ لِقَآءَنَا وَرَضُوا۟ بِٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱطْمَأَنُّوا۟ بِهَا وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا غَـٰفِلُونَ

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ لِقَآءَنَا—No doubt about it, those who are not hopeful for our meeting. Underline this phrase, because لَا يَرْجُونَ لِقَآءَنَا will come up over and over again in this Surah. These are people who don't hope to meet Allah ﷻ. That's the whole rationale behind seeking intercessors—"I'm not going to deal with Allah ﷻ directly, so let me find somebody in between."

Allah ﷻ is essentially exposing their thought process: these are the ones who have no hope of meeting Us.

وَرَضُوا۟ بِٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا—And they were content with worldly life. وَٱطْمَأَنُّوا۟ بِهَا—And they were completely tranquil with it, completely satisfied, fully at peace with this worldly life alone.

Think about this: How long do we actually live in this world? We spend a good ten, fifteen, maybe even twenty years just figuring out who we are, gaining consciousness, realizing what we're supposed to do with our lives. The moment we truly become adults with the strength and freedom to pursue our goals, we're immediately burdened with responsibilities. We spend the better part of our youth trying to gain some kind of stability—financial stability, career stability, family stability.

And by the time we finally achieve that stability, our backs start giving out. Our hair starts turning gray. Our eyesight weakens. And we start wondering: Is this why I was here? Is that all there is to life? Just play a little, work hard, get old and die?

Some people—and this is what Allah ﷻ is describing—are completely content with just that. When people claim that religious people are narrow-minded and they themselves are the broad-minded ones, look at how narrow their view of life actually is. What a limited, depressing view: this is it, this is all there is.

That's why you have midlife crises. That's why people fall into depression as they age, because there's nothing else for them. Their life is coming to an end and there was no greater purpose to any of it.

وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا غَـٰفِلُونَ—And those are the ones who are truly heedless of Our signs. If you have no preparation to make for the next life, and you're completely happy in this life, do you feel any need for Allah's ﷻ revelations? No. You don't even have to look for them. You can be completely oblivious that revelation even exists. You can live your entire life without knowing or feeling the need for it.

Why? Because the quest for revelation begins with a question: Is there something more than this life? But if there's nothing more for you than this life, then there's no reason to pursue that question at all.

translation: For those their refuge will be the Fire because of what they used to earn
commentary:
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَأْوَىٰهُمُ ٱلنَّارُ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَأْوَىٰهُمُ ٱلنَّارُ—Those people, their final place to return to, their dwelling, is the Fire. بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ—On account of what they continuously earned.

The word يَكْسِبُونَ indicates something ongoing, habitual. They kept earning this outcome through their choices, their priorities, their indifference to the signs of Allah ﷻ.

translation: Indeed, those who have believed and done righteous deeds - their Lord will guide them because of their faith. Beneath them rivers will flow in the Gardens of Pleasure
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ يَهْدِيهِمْ رَبُّهُم بِإِيمَـٰنِهِمْ ۖ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهِمُ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ فِى جَنَّـٰتِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ

Now comes the contrast. إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ—Those who truly believed and did the few good deeds expected of them. يَهْدِيهِمْ رَبُّهُم بِإِيمَـٰنِهِمْ—Their Master is going to guide them on account of their iman, because of their faith.

This is beautiful: iman itself becomes the means of further guidance. Faith leads to more faith, guidance leads to more guidance.

تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهِمُ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ—From beneath them rivers will be flowing. فِى جَنَّـٰتِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ—In gardens that are full of constant blessings.

The word نَعِيم means constant blessings. Here's something interesting to think about: we have na'eem in this world too—the sun, the moon, oxygen, food, clothes. But what happens when you have constant blessings? You get used to them. You don't wake up in the morning, look at the sunrise and say SubhanAllah with amazement every single time. You don't open your closet and marvel at how many shirts you have. You don't put your socks on and think, "How blessed I am that Allah ﷻ gave me these clothes."

You don't open the fridge or look at the fruit sitting on the kitchen counter and feel genuine wonder at the bananas. Why not? Because you're used to it. Constant blessings in this world lead to taking them for granted.

But in Jannah, the blessings are described as نَعِيم—constant—yet the people there never get bored, never become desensitized. How is that possible?

translation: Their call therein will be, "Exalted are You, O Allah," and their greeting therein will be, "Peace." And the last of their call will be, "Praise to Allah, Lord of the worlds
commentary:
دَعْوَىٰهُمْ فِيهَا سُبْحَـٰنَكَ ٱللَّهُمَّ وَتَحِيَّتُهُمْ فِيهَا سَلَـٰمٌ ۚ وَءَاخِرُ دَعْوَىٰهُمْ أَنِ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

This ayah answers that question beautifully.

دَعْوَىٰهُمْ فِيهَا سُبْحَـٰنَكَ ٱللَّهُمَّ—Their call in it will be: "How perfect You are, O Allah!" When someone says سُبْحَانَكَ, they're expressing amazement, wonder, awe. They're surrounded by constant blessings, and they remain constantly amazed. They never become bored or desensitized, because the perfection they witness continuously inspires them to declare the perfection of their Creator.

When I was young, I asked my mother a question about Jannah. I used to play soccer, so I wondered: if we're in Jannah and every shot I take is going to be a goal, won't that get boring? My mom said yes, every time—you won't miss. So I spent that whole summer thinking about it. How could it not be boring if you can't miss? If you play a video game with all the cheat codes activated and you can't die, it gets boring quickly.

But that's the miraculous nature of Jannah—you're constantly experiencing something so perfect that it generates fresh amazement every single time. سُبْحَـٰنَكَ ٱللَّهُمَّ—How perfect You are, O Allah!

وَتَحِيَّتُهُمْ فِيهَا سَلَـٰمٌ—And their greeting in it will be salam. The word تَحِيَّة comes from the root ح-ي-ي, which means life. To greet someone with tahiyyah originally meant to pray for their life to be preserved, to be prolonged. That's why we say اَلسَّلَامُ عَلَيْكُمْ—it's both a greeting and a prayer. حَيَّاكَ ٱللَّٰهُ means "May Allah ﷻ give you life, preserve your life."

In Jannah, the way people will pray for each other's life to be preserved is through the word سَلَـٰمٌ. And this is profound, because what is life without peace? Real life, meaningful life, is when you have peace. People living in chaos don't feel truly alive—they dream of a time when they can live in peace. They say, "If only this war would end, then we could have a life again." They literally think of life and peace together.

In Jannah, the greeting itself combines these concepts: تَحِيَّتُهُمْ فِيهَا سَلَـٰمٌ—their greeting will be peace, their prayer for each other will be peace, and in that peace they will find true, eternal life.

وَءَاخِرُ دَعْوَىٰهُمْ أَنِ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ—And the final of their calls will be: "All praise and gratitude belongs to Allah ﷻ, the Master of all nations."

This placement is beautiful. اَلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ is the first ayah of Surah Al-Fatihah, the Opening. So it's as if the people of Jannah are reminding themselves where their guidance began—with Allah's ﷻ Book. Every time they meet each other, they're saying سُبْحَـٰنَكَ ٱللَّهُمَّ, and every time they part with each other, they conclude with اَلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ.

Their beginning and their end is the praise of Allah ﷻ.

translation: And if Allah was to hasten for the people the evil [they invoke] as He hastens for them the good, their term would have been ended for them. But We leave the ones who do not expect the meeting with Us, in their transgression, wandering blindly
commentary:
۞ وَلَوْ يُعَجِّلُ ٱللَّهُ لِلنَّاسِ ٱلشَّرَّ ٱسْتِعْجَالَهُم بِٱلْخَيْرِ لَقُضِىَ إِلَيْهِمْ أَجَلُهُمْ ۖ فَنَذَرُ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ لِقَآءَنَا فِى طُغْيَـٰنِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ

This ayah addresses a fundamental human tendency: our impatience in asking for what we think is good for us. Allah ﷻ says that if He were to hasten the harm that people ask for in the same way they rush to seek good, their appointed time would have been sealed already.

The Arabic word تَعْجِيل means to rush something ahead of its appropriate time. This is different from استعجال, which simply means to hasten. When Allah ﷻ commands us to rush toward our prayers or good deeds, He uses استعجال because hastening toward goodness is praiseworthy. But تَعْجِيل carries the connotation of advancing something inappropriately, like taking medicine before its proper time or breaking your fast before sunset. The harm in this lies not in the speed itself, but in the violation of proper timing.

The disbelievers of Makkah used to mockingly challenge the Prophet ﷺ: "Bring the punishment you're warning us about! Let's see it!" In their arrogance, they thought the arrival of this punishment would somehow expose the Messenger ﷺ as false and end their troubles. In their minds, demanding this punishment was actually a good strategy. But Allah ﷻ reveals the reality: if He were to grant what they're asking for with the same immediacy with which they seek worldly good, their destruction would already be complete.

The ayah concludes with a powerful warning: فَنَذَرُ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ لِقَآءَنَا فِى طُغْيَـٰنِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ - "So We leave those who do not expect to meet Us, blindly wandering in their transgression." This phrase "those who do not hope to meet Us" appears for the first time in this passage, and it will repeat. These are people who have no expectation of standing before Allah ﷻ, no concern for accountability. The word يَعْمَهُونَ describes someone wandering blindly, moving forward without sight or direction. They continue in their rebellion, unable to see the path, unable to recognize the reality they're rushing toward.

translation: And when affliction touches man, he calls upon Us, whether lying on his side or sitting or standing; but when We remove from him his affliction, he continues [in disobedience] as if he had never called upon Us to [remove] an affliction that touched him. Thus is made pleasing to the transgressors that which they have been doing
commentary:
وَإِذَا مَسَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ ٱلضُّرُّ دَعَانَا لِجَنۢبِهِۦٓ أَوْ قَاعِدًا أَوْ قَآئِمًا فَلَمَّا كَشَفْنَا عَنْهُ ضُرَّهُۥ مَرَّ كَأَن لَّمْ يَدْعُنَآ إِلَىٰ ضُرٍّ مَّسَّهُۥ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ زُيِّنَ لِلْمُسْرِفِينَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ

This ayah moves from the collective to the individual, describing a pattern that cuts across humanity. When harm touches the human being, he calls on Us - lying on his side, sitting, or standing. The progression here captures someone in genuine distress, calling on Allah ﷻ in every position, unable to find rest or comfort. He's lying down, calling out. He sits up, still calling. He stands, continuing to call. The repetition emphasizes his desperation.

But then comes the shift: فَلَمَّا كَشَفْنَا عَنْهُ ضُرَّهُۥ - when We remove his harm from him. The word كَشَفْنَا is particularly meaningful. It comes from كَشْف, which means to unveil, to uncover, to remove a covering. The image is of ease that was hidden underneath a covering of difficulty. Allah ﷻ lifts that covering, and suddenly the person can breathe again.

What happens next? مَرَّ كَأَن لَّمْ يَدْعُنَآ - he moves on as though he had never called upon Us. The verb مَرَّ means to walk, to move forward, to pass by. There's something in the body language here. Picture someone weighed down by depression or difficulty - shoulders drooped, head down, movements slow. Now picture that same person when the problem is gone: shoulders back, head up, confident stride. He's walking around completely differently, as though the desperate prayers of moments ago never happened.

This isn't just about forgetting to say Alhamdulillah. This is about returning to the very indulgence that caused the problem in the first place. The ayah concludes: كَذَٰلِكَ زُيِّنَ لِلْمُسْرِفِينَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ - "Thus is made pleasing to the transgressors what they have been doing." The word مُسْرِفِينَ refers to those who go beyond limits, who overindulge. It was their crossing of boundaries that created the crisis to begin with.

Consider a contemporary example: Someone becomes addicted to watching inappropriate content online. Then they develop a serious eye condition, and the doctor warns they might lose their vision. Panic sets in. They immediately think of spiritual causes: "Allah ﷻ is punishing me for what I've been watching." Every streaming app gets deleted. YouTube is gone. The Netflix account is cancelled. They make sincere tawbah, promising never to return to those sins. But as the condition improves and the doctor gives them good news, slowly the apps get reinstalled. The accounts get reactivated. They're back to the same pattern, walking around as though that moment of crisis and sincere repentance never occurred. This is the cycle Allah ﷻ is describing - and it's a cycle He sees and knows completely.

translation: And We had already destroyed generations before you when they wronged, and their messengers had come to them with clear proofs, but they were not to believe. Thus do We recompense the criminal people
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ أَهْلَكْنَا ٱلْقُرُونَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ لَمَّا ظَلَمُوا۟ ۙ وَجَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ لِيُؤْمِنُوا۟ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ

The ayah now escalates from individual transgression to collective destruction. وَلَقَدْ أَهْلَكْنَا ٱلْقُرُونَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ - "And We certainly destroyed generations before you." The word قُرُون refers to multiple generations, communities, even civilizations. What might seem like isolated individual sins accumulate into the transgression of entire societies.

Someone might think, "I'm just one person sinning. Allah ﷻ isn't going to destroy an entire town because of me." But when everyone in that society thinks this way, when the collective attitude becomes one of dismissing accountability, that's precisely when divine punishment becomes inevitable. The individual choices compound into societal corruption.

These nations were destroyed لَمَّا ظَلَمُوا۟ - when they did wrong, when they oppressed. And it wasn't for lack of guidance: وَجَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ - their messengers came to them with clear proofs, undeniable evidence. Yet وَمَا كَانُوا۟ لِيُؤْمِنُوا۟ - they were not going to believe. They were so committed to their disbelief, so entrenched in their ways, that belief became impossible for them.

The ayah ends with a universal principle: كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ - "Thus do We recompense the criminal people." This is Allah's consistent way with those who persist in criminality despite clear warning and overwhelming evidence.

translation: Then We made you successors in the land after them so that We may observe how you will do
commentary:
ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَـٰكُمْ خَلَـٰٓئِفَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ لِنَنظُرَ كَيْفَ تَعْمَلُونَ

After describing the destruction of previous nations, Allah ﷻ addresses the current generation: ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَـٰكُمْ خَلَـٰٓئِفَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ - "Then We made you successors in the earth after them." The word خَلَـٰٓئِفَ means those who come after, those who inherit the position of others. The Makkans being addressed could see with their own eyes the ruins of more powerful civilizations - the remnants of 'Ad, Thamud, and others.

The message is sobering: You see what happened to them? They were stronger than you, more established, more numerous. We wiped them out. And now you've inherited their position on this earth.

But this inheritance isn't random or meaningless. لِنَنظُرَ كَيْفَ تَعْمَلُونَ - "So that We may see how you will act." This is your test. It's your turn now. What are you going to do with this opportunity? Will you learn from those who came before, or will you repeat their mistakes?

This principle applies to every generation. Each one inherits the earth from those before, and each one faces the same fundamental test: How will you act? What will you do with the time, resources, and opportunities you've been given?

translation: And when Our verses are recited to them as clear evidences, those who do not expect the meeting with Us say, "Bring us a Qur'an other than this or change it." Say, [O Muhammad], "It is not for me to change it on my own accord. I only follow what is revealed to me. Indeed I fear, if I should disobey my Lord, the punishment of a tremendous Day
commentary:
وَإِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَاتُنَا بَيِّنَـٰتٍ ۙ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ لِقَآءَنَا ٱئْتِ بِقُرْءَانٍ غَيْرِ هَـٰذَآ أَوْ بَدِّلْهُ ۚ قُلْ مَا يَكُونُ لِىٓ أَنْ أُبَدِّلَهُۥ مِن تِلْقَآئِ نَفْسِىٓ ۖ إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ ۖ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ إِنْ عَصَيْتُ رَبِّى عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ

When Our ayat are recited to them as clear proofs - بَيِّنَـٰتٍ, absolutely clear, leaving no room for doubt - those who do not hope to meet Us respond with a demand. This phrase appears for the third time now: ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ لِقَآءَنَا. It's being emphasized, repeated, identifying a particular mindset.

Their objection? ٱئْتِ بِقُرْءَانٍ غَيْرِ هَـٰذَآ أَوْ بَدِّلْهُ - "Bring a Quran other than this, or change it." They're essentially saying: "This message is too heavy. All this talk about Judgment Day, about accountability, about having no intercessors, about answering for our own deeds - it's too much responsibility. Can't you soften it a bit? Make it more palatable? Give us something easier to accept?"

The Prophet ﷺ is commanded to respond with absolute clarity: قُلْ مَا يَكُونُ لِىٓ أَنْ أُبَدِّلَهُۥ مِن تِلْقَآئِ نَفْسِىٓ - "Say: It is not for me to change it of my own accord." The phrase مِن تِلْقَآئِ نَفْسِى is significant. تِلْقَآء means something that originates entirely from oneself, with no outside influence or control. He's saying: "I have absolutely no authority to modify this message. It doesn't originate from me."

إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ - "I only follow what is revealed to me." His role is to follow, to convey, not to edit or customize the message according to people's preferences.

Then comes a remarkable statement: إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ إِنْ عَصَيْتُ رَبِّى عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ - "Indeed, I fear if I should disobey my Lord, the punishment of a tremendous Day." The Prophet ﷺ is telling them: "You're asking me to make these changes thinking it will make things easier for you. But I'm the one who's afraid. I'm afraid of what would happen to me if I were to disobey Allah ﷻ by changing His words. The fear belongs to me, not to you - and that should tell you something about your own state."

translation: Say, "If Allah had willed, I would not have recited it to you, nor would He have made it known to you, for I had remained among you a lifetime before it. Then will you not reason
commentary:
قُل لَّوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مَا تَلَوْتُهُۥ عَلَيْكُمْ وَلَآ أَدْرَىٰكُم بِهِۦ ۖ فَقَدْ لَبِثْتُ فِيكُمْ عُمُرًا مِّن قَبْلِهِۦٓ ۚ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ

The argument continues with a powerful proof. قُل لَّوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مَا تَلَوْتُهُۥ عَلَيْكُمْ - "Say: If Allah had willed, I would not have recited it to you." This Quran that you're objecting to - if Allah ﷻ hadn't willed it, you would never have received it in the first place.

وَلَآ أَدْرَىٰكُم بِهِۦ - "Nor would He have made it known to you." The verb أَدْرَىٰ is particularly nuanced. It comes from the family of words related to عِلْم, but أَدْرَىٰ specifically means to come to know something through clues and indications, not through direct statement. Like seeing smoke and concluding there's a fire - you didn't see the fire directly, but the evidence led you to that conclusion.

Allah ﷻ is saying: I wouldn't have given you direct revelation, nor would I have even provided you with the circumstantial evidence and clues that point to its truth. And what are those clues?

فَقَدْ لَبِثْتُ فِيكُمْ عُمُرًا مِّن قَبْلِهِۦٓ - "For I had remained among you a lifetime before it." The word لَبِثْتُ means to stay somewhere for an extended period, to be settled there with no plans of moving. "I've lived my entire life among you, in Makkah, settled here."

This is profound. The proof of the Quran's authenticity isn't just in its linguistic miracle or its legislative wisdom - it's in the character of the one conveying it. The Prophet ﷺ is being told to direct them to his own biography: "You know who I am. You've watched me for forty years. You gave me the titles Al-Ameen and As-Sadiq - the Trustworthy, the Truthful. I didn't give myself those titles. You did. Why would I suddenly start lying now?"

Notice that Allah ﷻ has to command him to say this. The Prophet ﷺ wouldn't point to his own character out of humility. But Allah ﷻ tells him: No, tell them. Make them confront this reality.

أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ - "Then will you not use your intellect?" Why would someone with an established reputation for honesty suddenly fabricate the most elaborate lie possible? What has he gained from this message? He used to have honor and status, and now he faces mockery and persecution. What wealth has he acquired? What praise does he receive now compared to before? Use your minds. Think about the motivation. Does any of this make sense if this were a lie?

This ayah establishes a crucial principle: The message of Islam cannot be separated from the character and credibility of those who carry it. Before people will receive the message, they need to see it embodied in a credible life. Our character, our ethics, our business practices, our dealings with society, the way we live as individuals and as a community - these are the first proofs of Islam. These establish credibility. Then, upon that foundation of trust and good character, we place the beauty of the message itself.

translation: So who is more unjust than he who invents a lie about Allah or denies His signs? Indeed, the criminals will not succeed
commentary:
فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا أَوْ كَذَّبَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ

After establishing the Prophet's credibility, Allah ﷻ poses a rhetorical question: فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا - "So who is more wrong than one who invents a lie about Allah?" The implication is clear: they're essentially accusing the Prophet ﷺ of fabricating revelation. But who could be a worse wrongdoer than someone who attributes lies to Allah ﷻ?

أَوْ كَذَّبَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِهِۦٓ - "Or denies His signs." There are two crimes here: fabricating lies against Allah ﷻ, and rejecting the truth when it comes from Him. Both represent ultimate forms of injustice.

إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ - "Indeed, the criminals will not succeed." لَا يُفْلِحُ - they will not achieve success, will not prosper, will not attain their goals. This is stated with certainty, with emphasis through إِنَّ.

This ayah also carries a warning for anyone who handles the Quran: lying against Allah ﷻ isn't limited to fabricating entirely new scripture. It includes misrepresenting what Allah ﷻ has said. When we interpret the Quran, we must do so with sincerity and care. Yes, mistakes in tafsir are possible - scholars differ, and that's understood. But what about intentionally twisting meanings? Interpreting ayahs in ways we know are incorrect because it pleases certain audiences, or because it avoids difficult truths, or because it makes people more comfortable?

Allah ﷻ doesn't just judge what comes out of our mouths. He knows what's in the heart. The intention behind how we present His revelation matters enormously. To deliberately misrepresent the Quran - even subtly, even with "good intentions" - falls under the umbrella of this severe warning.

translation: And they worship other than Allah that which neither harms them nor benefits them, and they say, "These are our intercessors with Allah " Say, "Do you inform Allah of something He does not know in the heavens or on the earth?" Exalted is He and high above what they associate with Him
commentary:
وَيَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَضُرُّهُمْ وَلَا يَنفَعُهُمْ وَيَقُولُونَ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ شُفَعَـٰٓؤُنَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ قُلْ أَتُنَبِّـُٔونَ ٱللَّهَ بِمَا لَا يَعْلَمُ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَلَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ وَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ

This ayah addresses the fundamental irrationality of shirk. وَيَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَضُرُّهُمْ وَلَا يَنفَعُهُمْ - "And they worship besides Allah that which neither harms them nor benefits them." The objects of their worship are completely powerless. They can't cause harm, and they can't bring benefit. They're utterly ineffectual.

Yet the mushrikoon have an explanation: وَيَقُولُونَ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ شُفَعَـٰٓؤُنَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ - "And they say: These are our intercessors with Allah." They conceptualize these idols and false deities as intermediaries, as advocates who will speak on their behalf before Allah ﷻ. "We don't worship them as ultimate gods," they might argue. "We worship them so they'll put in a good word for us with Allah ﷻ."

The response demolishes this logic: قُلْ أَتُنَبِّـُٔونَ ٱللَّهَ بِمَا لَا يَعْلَمُ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَلَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - "Say: Do you inform Allah of something He does not know in the heavens or the earth?" 

Think about what you're actually claiming. You're suggesting that on the Day of Judgment, these intercessors of yours will come before Allah ﷻ and say, "Wait, before You judge this person, You don't know the full story. Let me tell You about them." As if they possess information Allah ﷻ lacks. As if they can reveal to Him something hidden from His knowledge. Do you really think there's anything in the heavens or the earth that Allah ﷻ doesn't already know?

The absurdity is clear. This is like a defendant in court saying to the judge, "Wait, I have someone who will testify about evidence you've never seen." That might actually work in a human courtroom, because human judges have limited knowledge. But you're going to try that with Allah ﷻ? You think you're going to present Him with new information?

The ayah concludes with a powerful declaration: سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ وَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ - "Glorified and Exalted is He above what they associate with Him." سُبْحَـٰن expresses Allah's absolute perfection and freedom from any deficiency. تَعَـٰلَىٰ means He is elevated, raised high above. He is far above, infinitely beyond the flawed concepts and false associations they attribute to Him.

translation: And mankind was not but one community [united in religion], but [then] they differed. And if not for a word that preceded from your Lord, it would have been judged between them [immediately] concerning that over which they differ
commentary:
وَمَا كَانَ ٱلنَّاسُ إِلَّآ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً فَٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ ۚ وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِن رَّبِّكَ لَقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُمْ فِيمَا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ

This ayah provides historical perspective on the human condition. وَمَا كَانَ ٱلنَّاسُ إِلَّآ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً - "And mankind was not but one community." Originally, humanity existed in a state of unity. There was one understanding, one way, one belief system.

فَٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ - "Then they differed." What happened? They fell into disagreement. The divergence came later. The splintering into different religions, different ideologies, different truth claims - this wasn't the original state. It was deviation from an original unity.

وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِن رَّبِّكَ - "And if not for a word that preceded from your Lord..." This refers to Allah's decree that judgment will be delayed until an appointed time. The word كَلِمَةٌ here means a divine decree, a decision that has already been made.

لَقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُمْ فِيمَا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ - "It would have been judged between them concerning that in which they differ." If it weren't for this predetermined delay, the matter would have been settled immediately. Everyone who deviated from the truth would have been dealt with right away. But Allah ﷻ, in His wisdom, has ordained that people will be given time, that the final judgment will come at an appointed hour, not immediately upon their transgression.

This is both a mercy and a warning. It's a mercy because it gives people time to return, to recognize their error, to find their way back to the truth. But it's also a warning: don't mistake the delay for indifference. The judgment is coming. The settling of accounts is inevitable.

translation: And they say, "Why is a sign not sent down to him from his Lord?" So say, "The unseen is only for Allah [to administer], so wait; indeed, I am with you among those who wait
commentary:
وَيَقُولُونَ لَوْلَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ ۖ فَقُلْ إِنَّمَا ٱلْغَيْبُ لِلَّهِ فَٱنتَظِرُوٓا۟ إِنِّى مَعَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْمُنتَظِرِينَ

The disbelievers continue their objections: وَيَقُولُونَ لَوْلَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ - "And they say: Why has a sign not been sent down to him from his Lord?" They want something more than the Quran. They want a physical miracle, something spectacular and undeniable that will force belief. "Just words aren't enough," they're essentially saying. "Show us something miraculous."

The Prophet ﷺ is instructed to respond: فَقُلْ إِنَّمَا ٱلْغَيْبُ لِلَّهِ - "So say: The unseen is only for Allah." What sign will come, when it will come, what form it will take - all of this belongs to the realm of the unseen, and the unseen is entirely within Allah's domain and knowledge, not yours or mine.

فَٱنتَظِرُوٓا۟ - "So wait." You want a sign? Then wait for it. But here's what makes this response remarkable: إِنِّى مَعَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْمُنتَظِرِينَ - "Indeed, I am with you among those who wait."

This is actually threatening language, though it might not sound like it at first. The Prophet ﷺ is saying: "You want to see what happens next? Fine. I'm waiting right alongside you. Let's see how this plays out." It's like someone saying, "You want to see who's right? Let's wait and find out together."

The calm confidence in this response is striking. There's no anxiety, no desperation to prove himself. Just: "The unseen belongs to Allah ﷻ. We'll both wait. I'm waiting with you."

translation: And when We give the people a taste of mercy after adversity has touched them, at once they conspire against Our verses. Say, "Allah is swifter in strategy." Indeed, Our messengers record that which you conspire
commentary:
وَإِذَآ أَذَقْنَا ٱلنَّاسَ رَحْمَةً مِّنۢ بَعْدِ ضَرَّآءَ مَسَّتْهُمْ إِذَا لَهُم مَّكْرٌ فِىٓ ءَايَاتِنَا ۚ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ أَسْرَعُ مَكْرًا ۚ إِنَّ رُسُلَنَا يَكْتُبُونَ مَا تَمْكُرُونَ

This ayah returns to a theme introduced earlier in this passage, bringing the discussion full circle. وَإِذَآ أَذَقْنَا ٱلنَّاسَ رَحْمَةً مِّنۢ بَعْدِ ضَرَّآءَ مَسَّتْهُمْ - "And when We let the people taste mercy after adversity has touched them..." This echoes the earlier description of the person who calls on Allah ﷻ in distress, then forgets Him when relief comes.

But here the focus is on what happens after that relief: إِذَا لَهُم مَّكْرٌ فِىٓ ءَايَاتِنَا - "Immediately they have plots concerning Our signs." The word مَكْر refers to a secret plan, a covert scheme designed to deceive an enemy and bring them to harm. It's strategic deception.

Remember the example given earlier? Someone comes to the Prophet ﷺ with a problem, receives help, experiences Allah's mercy through that help - and then turns around and plots against the Messenger ﷺ. They go from seeking divine assistance to scheming against divine guidance.

The response? قُلِ ٱللَّهُ أَسْرَعُ مَكْرًا - "Say: Allah is swifter in strategy." But notice the word used here: أَسْرَعُ. This is from the root of سُرْعَة, meaning speed or quickness. Earlier in this passage, when describing inappropriate rushing, the word used was تَعْجِيل. But here, Allah ﷻ uses أَسْرَعُ for Himself, which simply means "faster" or "quicker" without the connotation of inappropriateness.

Allah ﷻ is saying: You think you're being clever with your secret plans? I'm faster. My response is swifter than your schemes. And unlike your makr, Mine is always appropriate, always just, always perfectly timed.

Then comes the final reminder: إِنَّ رُسُلَنَا يَكْتُبُونَ مَا تَمْكُرُونَ - "Indeed, Our messengers are recording that which you plot." The angels are writing it all down. By definition, makr is supposed to be secret - hidden schemes, covert plans. But Allah ﷻ is saying: Your secrets aren't secret. I have a complete transcript of everything you've been plotting.

The closing of this passage returns us to divine knowledge and divine record-keeping. You might think your plans are hidden, your schemes unknown. But every thought, every intention, every whispered conspiracy is recorded. The angels have it all written down. When the time comes for judgment, nothing will have been forgotten, nothing overlooked. The books will be opened, and the full accounting will be made.

translation: It is He who enables you to travel on land and sea until, when you are in ships and they sail with them by a good wind and they rejoice therein, there comes a storm wind and the waves come upon them from everywhere and they assume that they are surrounded, supplicating Allah, sincere to Him in religion, "If You should save us from this, we will surely be among the thankful
commentary:
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُسَيِّرُكُمْ فِى ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا كُنتُمْ فِى ٱلْفُلْكِ وَجَرَيْنَ بِهِم بِرِيحٍ طَيِّبَةٍ وَفَرِحُوا۟ بِهَا جَآءَتْهَا رِيحٌ عَاصِفٌ وَجَآءَهُمُ ٱلْمَوْجُ مِن كُلِّ مَكَانٍ وَظَنُّوٓا۟ أَنَّهُمْ أُحِيطَ بِهِمْ ۙ دَعَوُا۟ ٱللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ لَئِنْ أَنجَيْتَنَا مِنْ هَـٰذِهِۦ لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ

This ayah continues exploring the nature of people who find themselves in trouble, turn to Allah ﷻ for help, and then the moment their difficulty passes, return to their evil ways. The previous passages discussed a particular incident where someone came to the Prophet ﷺ seeking guidance, only to become his enemy again once his problem was solved. Now Allah ﷻ generalizes this pattern through a powerful image that appears multiple times throughout the Quran: the experience of people at sea.

To appreciate this imagery fully, we need to understand something about seafaring. Ships that sail into the middle of the ocean reach a point where they truly belong only to Allah ﷻ. Even experienced sailors recognize this reality. The moment a ship leaves the docks and can no longer see land in any direction, when storms arise and waves crash from all sides, everyone aboard knows that only Allah ﷻ can help them now.

The word يُسَيِّرُكُمْ comes from the root that means to take someone around, to help them navigate from place to place. Allah ﷻ says He is the One who takes you around فِى ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ (on land and at sea). The ayah then paints a vivid scene: people boarding massive ships, so large that the feminine plural is used to describe them. These aren't small vessels—they're carrying many passengers, and now they've left the docks and are sailing away.

وَجَرَيْنَ بِهِم بِرِيحٍ طَيِّبَةٍ (and they sailed with them by a good wind). In the days before steam engines and modern navigation, sailors depended entirely on wind. A favorable wind heading in their desired direction brought tremendous relief and joy. The scene is beautiful: sunny skies, passengers standing on deck feeling the pleasant breeze, watching the ship glide smoothly and swiftly through the water. Flags wave, people celebrate the journey ahead.

وَفَرِحُوا۟ بِهَا (and they rejoiced because of it). The word فَرِحُوا indicates being overjoyed, overwhelmed with happiness. This type of excessive joy is generally not viewed positively in the Quran, as it suggests losing control of oneself in happiness. Interestingly, there's only one place in the entire Quran where this intense joy is used in a positive sense, and it appears later in this very surah.

Then, without warning, the narrative shifts dramatically. Notice there's no transition, no connecting word—suddenly جَآءَتْهَا رِيحٌ عَاصِفٌ (a fierce wind came upon it). The word عَاصِفٌ describes a wind that's inconsiderate, merciless, tearing through everything in its path without care for what it destroys. One moment there's a gentle breeze and clear skies, the next moment a violent storm appears.

Notice something subtle here: the word رِيحٌ (wind) is feminine in Arabic, but the adjective عَاصِفٌ is masculine. While Arabic allows some flexibility with natural phenomena, using the unexpected masculine form signals that this wind itself is abnormal, unexpected, unnatural—just as shocking as the grammatical choice itself.

وَجَآءَهُمُ ٱلْمَوْجُ مِن كُلِّ مَكَانٍ (and there came upon them waves from every place). Not just waves, but مَوْج—massive, towering waves attacking from all directions. The passengers don't know where the next one will strike. Will it slam into the ship from behind? From the front? From the sides? The unpredictability amplifies the terror.

وَظَنُّوٓا۟ أَنَّهُمْ أُحِيطَ بِهِمْ (and they were certain that they were surrounded). The camera angle, if you will, shifts to capture the realization on their faces—they're going to be overcome. This is the end. The narrative takes us from the celebratory dock scene, to the pleasant sailing, to the storm's sudden arrival, and now onto the ship itself, focusing on the passengers' terrified faces.

دَعَوُا۟ ٱللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ (they called upon Allah, sincere to Him in religion). In that moment of absolute desperation, they turn to Allah ﷻ with complete sincerity. This phrase مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ captures the purity of their call—no pretense, no show, no divided loyalty. Just pure, desperate supplication to the One who can actually save them.

لَئِنْ أَنجَيْتَنَا مِنْ هَـٰذِهِۦ لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ (If You save us from this, we will surely be among the thankful). The intensity of their promise is captured in the grammar—this isn't polite language. Nobody on a sinking ship says, "Verily, we shall be from the grateful." They're crying out, swearing, making solemn vows: "I'll be different! I won't be who I was! I swear I'll be grateful!"

This spiritual experience of a close call—whether on a turbulent flight, during a near-accident on the highway, or any moment when death feels imminent—brings people closer to Allah ﷻ. It's a universal human response. Even those who spent their lives denying Him find themselves calling out to Him when all other options disappear.

translation: But when He saves them, at once they commit injustice upon the earth without right. O mankind, your injustice is only against yourselves, [being merely] the enjoyment of worldly life. Then to Us is your return, and We will inform you of what you used to do
commentary:
فَلَمَّآ أَنجَىٰهُمْ إِذَا هُمْ يَبْغُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ ۗ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ إِنَّمَا بَغْيُكُمْ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِكُم ۖ مَّتَـٰعَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ ثُمَّ إِلَيْنَا مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَنُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ

فَلَمَّآ أَنجَىٰهُمْ إِذَا هُمْ يَبْغُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ (But when He saved them, at once they transgressed upon the earth without right). The moment Allah ﷻ rescues them, they immediately return to transgression. The word بَغْي is particularly meaningful—it literally means trying to grab more than your fair share, and in doing so, taking what belongs to others. It's rebellion that comes from greed, from wanting more than you deserve.

This isn't just about the people on that ship anymore. Allah ﷻ broadens the address: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ (O humanity). This pattern applies universally. Think about executives taking multimillion-dollar compensation packages while others struggle. Think about anyone using their position to take more than their rightful share. This is بَغْي—transgression born from greed.

إِنَّمَا بَغْيُكُمْ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِكُم (your transgression is only against yourselves). This phrase deserves to be underlined and memorized. It's one of those profound expressions you can apply throughout life. By definition, transgression harms others—you're taking someone else's share. But Allah ﷻ reveals the deeper reality: the real consequence falls on you. You think you're harming others, but you're actually destroying yourself.

مَّتَـٰعَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا (brief enjoyment of worldly life). The structure here is dismissive—notice it's in the accusative case, as if to say, "What are you so obsessed with? Just some temporary things you'll use in this worldly life?" The tone conveys how trivial these pursuits really are.

ثُمَّ إِلَيْنَا مَرْجِعُكُمْ (then to Us is your return). This phrase echoes throughout this surah, a constant reminder that every journey ends in the same place—returning to Allah ﷻ.

فَنُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (and We will inform you of what you used to do). The word نُنَبِّئُ means to thoroughly inform someone, to sit them down and go through everything in explicit detail. Imagine being called into the principal's office or sitting before your parents as they go through every single thing you did wrong. You keep sinking in your chair, wishing it would end, but they keep going, listing every detail. It's deeply humiliating. That's the image here—Allah ﷻ will thoroughly inform you, charge by charge, of everything you did.

translation: The example of [this] worldly life is but like rain which We have sent down from the sky that the plants of the earth absorb - [those] from which men and livestock eat - until, when the earth has taken on its adornment and is beautified and its people suppose that they have capability over it, there comes to it Our command by night or by day, and We make it as a harvest, as if it had not flourished yesterday. Thus do We explain in detail the signs for a people who give thought
commentary:
إِنَّمَا مَثَلُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا كَمَآءٍ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَٱخْتَلَطَ بِهِۦ نَبَاتُ ٱلْأَرْضِ مِمَّا يَأْكُلُ ٱلنَّاسُ وَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمُ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَآ أَخَذَتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ زُخْرُفَهَا وَٱزَّيَّنَتْ وَظَنَّ أَهْلُهَآ أَنَّهُمْ قَـٰدِرُونَ عَلَيْهَآ أَتَىٰهَآ أَمْرُنَا لَيْلًا أَوْ نَهَارًا فَجَعَلْنَـٰهَا حَصِيدًا كَأَن لَّمْ تَغْنَ بِٱلْأَمْسِ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ

إِنَّمَا مَثَلُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا (the example of worldly life is only). Now Allah ﷻ presents a parable that captures the temporary, deceptive nature of this worldly existence.

كَمَآءٍ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ (like water which We have sent down from the sky). Rain descends from the heavens, that life-giving water that everyone depends upon.

فَٱخْتَلَطَ بِهِۦ نَبَاتُ ٱلْأَرْضِ (and the plants of the earth become mixed with it). The word ٱخْتَلَطَ indicates different ingredients, components, and elements interacting and mixing together. The water penetrates the soil, which contains various nutrients, seeds, and minerals. They all interact with each other—the water, the soil, the seeds—engaging in this complex dance of growth.

مِمَّا يَأْكُلُ ٱلنَّاسُ وَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمُ (from that which people and grazing livestock eat). Both humans and animals depend on what grows from this interaction between rain and earth. The crops emerge, the vegetation appears, life flourishes.

حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَآ أَخَذَتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ زُخْرُفَهَا (until when the earth takes on its adornment). The word زُخْرُف is fascinating—it refers to superficial beauty, the kind that catches the eye but lacks substance. It's used for makeup, glittery clothing, shiny jewelry, bright colors designed to attract attention. It's also used for embellished speech, when someone is particularly good with words and can talk their way out of trouble. In all cases, it describes beauty that's impressive on the surface but has no depth.

Allah ﷻ describes the earth holding onto its adornments like a bride decorated for her wedding—the farm blossoms, flowers bloom in various colors and textures, crops grow lush and full.

وَٱزَّيَّنَتْ (and is beautified). The earth becomes thoroughly decorated and adorned.

وَظَنَّ أَهْلُهَآ أَنَّهُمْ قَـٰدِرُونَ عَلَيْهَآ (and its people suppose that they have capability over it). The word قَـٰدِرُونَ encompasses multiple meanings: having control, having something figured out, having the calculation right, being fully capable. The farmers look at their land and feel completely in charge. They've got it all figured out. Everything is under control. The crop is perfect, ready for harvest. In modern Arabic, we still use this word for capability—"I'm capable" or "I can't handle that."

The land reaches a state where it doesn't need anything more. No more watering, no more sunshine, no more tending. That's why wealthy people are called غني—they don't need anything else. Allah ﷻ calls Himself الغني because He needs no one. Similarly, this land has reached the point where it's غانية—completely self-sufficient, needing no more help.

أَتَىٰهَآ أَمْرُنَا لَيْلًا أَوْ نَهَارًا (Our command came to it by night or by day). Without warning, Allah's decree arrives. The timing is deliberately vague—was it night? Was it day? It doesn't matter. The point is the suddenness, the unexpectedness.

فَجَعَلْنَـٰهَا حَصِيدًا (and We made it as a harvest reaped). The word حَصِيد refers to what's left after harvest—the stubble, the bottom part of the plant that even the machinery can't reach, the part too low to be useful. In an instant, the flourishing farm is reduced to nothing but useless stubble.

كَأَن لَّمْ تَغْنَ بِٱلْأَمْسِ (as if it had not flourished yesterday). This phrase is absolutely powerful. Looking at that devastated land, you'd never imagine that just the day before—or even hours before—it was perfect, lush, self-sufficient. The transformation is so complete that it's as though the previous state never existed.

كَذَٰلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ (Thus do We explain in detail the signs for a people who give thought). This is how Allah ﷻ thoroughly explains His ayat—His revelations, His signs—for people who want to engage in deep, careful contemplation.

The parable draws a parallel to the situation of the Quraysh and all who feel secure in their worldly power. You think you're in control? You have wealth, influence, military might? People respect you, fear you? Who could possibly mess with you? But Allah ﷻ can change your situation overnight, just as He can destroy a perfect farm in an instant. There is no stability, no true security in this worldly life. Nothing here is permanent. The only real peace, the only genuine stability, exists in the next life.

translation: And Allah invites to the Home of Peace and guides whom He wills to a straight path
commentary:
وَٱللَّهُ يَدْعُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ دَارِ ٱلسَّلَـٰمِ وَيَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ

وَٱللَّهُ يَدْعُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ دَارِ ٱلسَّلَـٰمِ (And Allah invites to the Home of Peace). After describing the instability and deceptive nature of worldly life—both the storm at sea and the destroyed farm—Allah ﷻ presents the alternative. Just as that farm offered no real stability, nothing in this temporary life provides true security. But Allah ﷻ is calling you to something real: دَارِ ٱلسَّلَـٰمِ, the Home of Peace.

This is the only place where you can truly be at peace, where genuine safety exists. Unlike the fleeting security people feel when their ships sail smoothly or their farms flourish, this peace is eternal and unshakeable. Allah ﷻ isn't just offering it—He's actively inviting you, calling you toward it.

وَيَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (and He guides whom He wills to a straight path). The invitation is universal, but guidance to the صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (straight path) comes by Allah's will. This path leads directly to that Home of Peace, without deviation, without the turbulence of worldly storms or the sudden devastation that overtakes temporary success.

The contrast is profound: this world offers only temporary enjoyment that can vanish in an instant, while Allah ﷻ offers a permanent home of peace. The choice before humanity is between chasing the illusion of worldly stability and accepting Allah's invitation to genuine, eternal peace.

translation: For them who have done good is the best [reward] and extra. No darkness will cover their faces, nor humiliation. Those are companions of Paradise; they will abide therein eternally
commentary:
۞ لِّلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا۟ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ وَزِيَادَةٌ ۖ وَلَا يَرْهَقُ وُجُوهَهُمْ قَتَرٌ وَلَا ذِلَّةٌ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ

For those who excelled in this life, Allah ﷻ promises الْحُسْنَىٰ- the very best - وَزِيَادَةٌ- and then some. This word ziyadah carries something beautiful. Allah ﷻ describes the rewards of the people of Jannah with very few words in the Quran, but what little He mentions is enough. This is from the balagha, the eloquence of the Quran - making much meaning with minimal words.

The word الْحُسْنَىٰ is in the superlative form, which means "the very, very best." Once you reach the superlative, there's no going beyond it in our understanding. Above "better" is "best," and that's where our imagination stops. But Allah ﷻ says those who excelled will have the best - that's the limit of our imagination - and then He adds وَزِيَادَةٌ, "and even more than that." There's actually more beyond what you can conceive.

Then Allah ﷻ describes what they won't experience: وَلَا يَرْهَقُ وُجُوهَهُمْ قَتَرٌ- their faces will not be covered with qatar. The verb رَهَقَ means to overcast something forcefully, like forcefully hooding someone. قَتَرٌ refers to black smoke that comes from fire. Allah ﷻ is saying: you're going to have the very best of Jannah, beyond imagination - wa ziyadah - and here's something remarkable: you won't burn in hellfire. Allah ﷻ describes not going to hell as part of the reward itself.

The sequence here is significant. Usually in the Quran, things go from bad to worse or better to best, ending on a climactic note. Here, the smoke is bad, but what's mentioned next is even more intense: وَلَا ذِلَّةٌ - and not humiliation. The humiliation is presented as worse than the smoke. أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ- those are the people of Jannah هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ- in which they will remain.

translation: But they who have earned [blame for] evil doings - the recompense of an evil deed is its equivalent, and humiliation will cover them. They will have from Allah no protector. It will be as if their faces are covered with pieces of the night - so dark [are they]. Those are the companions of the Fire; they will abide therein eternally
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَسَبُوا۟ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ جَزَآءُ سَيِّئَةٍۭ بِمِثْلِهَا وَتَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ ۖ مَّا لَهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِنْ عَاصِمٍ ۖ كَأَنَّمَآ أُغْشِيَتْ وُجُوهُهُمْ قِطَعًا مِّنَ ٱلَّيْلِ مُظْلِمًا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ

Those who earned continual sins, multiple sins - جَزَآءُ سَيِّئَةٍۭ بِمِثْلِهَا the compensation for any sin is going to be its equivalent. وَتَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ- and humiliation will be covered over them, will overshadow them, will mask them.

مَّا لَهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِنْ عَاصِمٍ- they're not going to find in Allah ﷻ any protector whatsoever. They're hoping for protection from Allah ﷻ, but they're not going to find it. The word عَاصِمٍ alludes to someone holding you back, someone who grabs you to prevent you from harm - like when you're walking and don't realize there's a fifty-foot drop ahead, and your friend grabs you and holds you back from the danger you were about to fall into. Or when a child does something dangerous near a pool and the mother grabs him and doesn't let go. That's 'asim - holding onto someone for their protection. But these people will not find any 'asim whatsoever.

Notice the structure here: مَا لَهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِنْ عَاصِمٍ- the pronoun "them" is mentioned, but this actually alludes to its opposite. Who will have Allah ﷻ as their 'asim? The believers, because no qatar will be covering their faces, no dhillah will be covering their faces.

كَأَنَّمَآ أُغْشِيَتْ وُجُوهُهُمْ قِطَعًا مِّنَ ٱلَّيْلِ مُظْلِمًا- it is as though their faces have been covered with pieces of night, dark night. The word أُغْشِيَتْ means to cover something, but not completely - to cover it in a way that it's somewhat exposed. So their faces are almost completely covered, as if a piece of the black patch of the sky has been placed onto their face, making it dark. أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ- those are the people of the Fire هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ- in which they will remain. May Allah ﷻ not make us of them.

translation: And [mention, O Muhammad], the Day We will gather them all together - then We will say to those who associated others with Allah, "[Remain in] your place, you and your 'partners.' " Then We will separate them, and their "partners" will say, "You did not used to worship us
commentary:
وَيَوْمَ نَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعًا ثُمَّ نَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ مَكَانَكُمْ أَنتُمْ وَشُرَكَآؤُكُمْ ۚ فَزَيَّلْنَا بَيْنَهُمْ ۖ وَقَالَ شُرَكَآؤُهُم مَّا كُنتُمْ إِيَّانَا تَعْبُدُونَ

On that Day when We will gather all of them altogether, ثُمَّ نَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ - then We will say to those who committed shirk: مَكَانَكُمْ (- stay in your place.

Notice the language here. Allah ﷻ doesn't say "qumu makanakum" (stand in your place) - He just says مَكَانَكُمْ. When an authority is angry, he doesn't speak in complete sentences. He just uses one word that says everything. It's like during an exam when students are talking to each other and the teacher just says "Exam!" - one word, and everyone understands. These people are being herded, terrified, waiting in a stampede of people moving around, and suddenly they're told: Stay in your place. مَكَانَكُمْ أَنتُمْ وَشُرَكَآؤُكُمْ- you and your associates.

Who are these associates? Some scholars derive from this that the idols themselves will be thrown into the fire. Others say these are people like 'Isa (AS) and the scholars whose worship was sought, the saints and righteous people who may have been worshiped after their death. Why would Allah ﷻ call them like that? To show the harshness and sternness of this Day. And why is that important? Because this is the ultimate form of psychological torture. This person had all his hopes and aspirations with these so-called partners, and now he's standing next to them thinking, "Aren't you supposed to save me? You're in trouble too - you were talking big, what happened now?"

فَزَيَّلْنَا بَيْنَهُمْ- then everything between them has been canceled out. We made everything severed between them. وَقَالَ شُرَكَآؤُهُم مَّا كُنتُمْ إِيَّانَا تَعْبُدُونَ- and their associates, these innocent ones that were worshiped who didn't even know after their death that they were being worshiped, they're going to look at them and say: "You were worshiping us? We had nothing to do with this." They're in complete denial.

translation: And sufficient is Allah as a witness between us and you that we were of your worship unaware
commentary:
فَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ شَهِيدًۢا بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَكُمْ إِن كُنَّا عَنْ عِبَادَتِكُمْ لَغَـٰفِلِينَ

فَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ شَهِيدًۢا بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَكُمْ- Allah ﷻ is enough as a witness between us and yourselves. إِن كُنَّا عَنْ عِبَادَتِكُمْ لَغَـٰفِلِينَ- indeed we were absolutely unaware of the worship you were doing. The إِن here is actually a shortened form of إِنَّا, meaning "indeed, truly." They're saying: We had no idea people could be that foolish. They're completely disconnecting themselves from the people who used to have all their hopes and aspirations tied to them.

translation: There, [on that Day], every soul will be put to trial for what it did previously, and they will be returned to Allah, their master, the Truth, and lost from them is whatever they used to invent
commentary:
هُنَالِكَ تَبْلُوا۟ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّآ أَسْلَفَتْ ۚ وَرُدُّوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَوْلَىٰهُمُ ٱلْحَقِّ ۖ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ

هُنَالِكَ- then and there تَبْلُوا۟ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ- every person is going to be put to the test مَّآ أَسْلَفَتْ- whatever they had done in the past. أَسْلَفَتْ means what came in the past, what already occurred. The word also implies it's already done - the only time you'll have to deal with it is in the future. It's already happened in the past, so you can't change it now. Now you just have to deal with its consequences.

وَرُدُّوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ- and they will all be turned back toward Allah ﷻ, مَوْلَىٰهُمُ ٱلْحَقِّ- their Protector, their Guardian, their ultimate Authority, the True One. وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ- and everything they used to make up has been lost to them. They can't find it. The word ضَلَّ means something you really wanted is lost - you can't find it anywhere. Whatever they used to fabricate has completely gone to waste.

translation: Say, "Who provides for you from the heaven and the earth? Or who controls hearing and sight and who brings the living out of the dead and brings the dead out of the living and who arranges [every] matter?" They will say, "Allah," so say, "Then will you not fear Him
commentary:
قُلْ مَن يَرْزُقُكُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ أَمَّن يَمْلِكُ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَٱلْأَبْصَـٰرَ وَمَن يُخْرِجُ ٱلْحَىَّ مِنَ ٱلْمَيِّتِ وَيُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَيِّتَ مِنَ ٱلْحَىِّ وَمَن يُدَبِّرُ ٱلْأَمْرَ ۚ فَسَيَقُولُونَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ فَقُلْ أَفَلَا تَتَّقُونَ

قُلْ- tell them: مَن يَرْزُقُكُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ- who provides for you from the skies and the earth? أَمَّن يَمْلِكُ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَٱلْأَبْصَـٰرَ- or who is the one who owns hearing and seeing? 

وَمَن يُخْرِجُ ٱلْحَىَّ مِنَ ٱلْمَيِّتِ- and who brings out the living from the dead وَيُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَيِّتَ مِنَ ٱلْحَىِّ- and brings out the dead from the living? وَمَن يُدَبِّرُ ٱلْأَمْرَ- and who plans it all out, who controls the ultimate decision?

This is a reinforcement of what came at the very beginning of the Surah. فَسَيَقُولُونَ ٱللَّهُ- then they're going to turn around and say "Allah ﷻ." 

Wait - they don't worship other than Allah ﷻ? They do hold others as shuraka' (partners), but they hope these partners can help them on the Day of Resurrection, for salvation. That in itself is shirk. But notice: they don't say "we don't acknowledge Allah ﷻ" or "we don't acknowledge His divine power."

فَقُلْ أَفَلَا تَتَّقُونَ- then say: why don't you then have consciousness of Allah ﷻ? Why don't you want to protect yourselves?

translation: For that is Allah, your Lord, the Truth. And what can be beyond truth except error? So how are you averted
commentary:
فَذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ ۖ فَمَاذَا بَعْدَ ٱلْحَقِّ إِلَّا ٱلضَّلَـٰلُ ۖ فَأَنَّىٰ تُصْرَفُونَ

فَذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ- that is Allah ﷻ, your Master, truly. فَمَاذَا بَعْدَ ٱلْحَقِّ إِلَّا ٱلضَّلَـٰلُ- then after being exposed to the truth, what's left for you except misguidance? After you've been shown the truth in this way and you still reject it, there's no bright future left for you.

فَأَنَّىٰ تُصْرَفُونَ- then how come you're turned away? How is it that you're going in any other direction?

translation: Thus the word of your Lord has come into effect upon those who defiantly disobeyed - that they will not believe
commentary:
كَذَٰلِكَ حَقَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ فَسَقُوٓا۟ أَنَّهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ

كَذَٰلِكَ حَقَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ- that is how the word of your Master was manifested, became reality عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ فَسَقُوٓا۟- against those who engaged in corruption.

What these ayahs suggest is this: when you're exposed to the truth in the most convincing way, and Allah ﷻ is satisfied that the case has been made against you, and you still turn away - then at that point, the decision is: My job is done. The case has been made against you. Now whatever happens to you is rightful. Whatever happens to you is your own doing at this point.

أَنَّهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ- they are not going to believe. As a result of them not believing, that is why the word of Allah ﷻ - meaning the decision of Allah ﷻ, not revelation but His decree - became manifest upon them. When the Quran says "had the word not come before, the matter would have been decided," it means had the decision not been issued beforehand, they would have been dealt with immediately. But now they have deserved the final verdict.

translation: Say, "Are there of your 'partners' any who begins creation and then repeats it?" Say, "Allah begins creation and then repeats it, so how are you deluded
commentary:
قُلْ هَلْ مِن شُرَكَآئِكُم مَّن يَبْدَؤُا۟ ٱلْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُۥ ۚ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ يَبْدَؤُا۟ ٱلْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُۥ ۖ فَأَنَّىٰ تُؤْفَكُونَ

قُلْ هَلْ مِن شُرَكَآئِكُم مَّن يَبْدَؤُا۟ ٱلْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُۥ- tell them: is there any of your associates that starts off creation from nothing, then brings it back again?

قُلِ ٱللَّهُ يَبْدَؤُا۟ ٱلْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُۥ- tell them: Allah ﷻ starts off creation and then brings it back again. 

فَأَنَّىٰ تُؤْفَكُونَ- then how is it that you're taken in by lies? The word تُؤْفَكُونَ means to be deceived, to have lies fabricated and presented to you in a way that you accept them.

translation: Say, "Are there of your 'partners' any who guides to the truth?" Say, "Allah guides to the truth. So is He who guides to the truth more worthy to be followed or he who guides not unless he is guided? Then what is [wrong] with you - how do you judge
commentary:
قُلْ هَلْ مِن شُرَكَآئِكُم مَّن يَهْدِىٓ إِلَى ٱلْحَقِّ ۚ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ يَهْدِى لِلْحَقِّ ۗ أَفَمَن يَهْدِىٓ إِلَى ٱلْحَقِّ أَحَقُّ أَن يُتَّبَعَ أَمَّن لَّا يَهِدِّىٓ إِلَّآ أَن يُهْدَىٰ ۖ فَمَا لَكُمْ كَيْفَ تَحْكُمُونَ

قُلْ هَلْ مِن شُرَكَآئِكُم مَّن يَهْدِىٓ إِلَى ٱلْحَقِّ ask them: are there any of your associates who can guide to the truth?

The word يَهْدِىٓ إِلَى means to give directions to something. "Could you guide me to the masjid?" means give me directions to the masjid. So هَدَى إِلَى means to give directions, to point toward something.

قُلِ ٱللَّهُ يَهْدِى لِلْحَقِّ- tell them: Allah ﷻ guides to the truth. But notice the preposition changed - it's not إِلَىanymore, it's لِ. 

In Arabic, هَدَى with the preposition لِ means to guide someone all the way to their destination, not just give them directions but take them there. The truth is both a path and a destination - the Sirat al-Mustaqeem is the truth, and what leads to Jannah is also the truth. Both are truth. But these false partners can't even guide you to the path, can't even give you directions. Allah ﷻ gets you all the way to the destination.

That's why when we reach the final truth, which is Jannah, we say الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي هَدَانَا لِهَـٰذَا- all praise belongs to Allah ﷻ, the One who guided us all the way to this. He got us all the way there.

أَفَمَن يَهْدِىٓ إِلَى ٱلْحَقِّ- as for the one who guides to the truth. Now إِلَى is used again, referring to the Prophet ﷺ who gives direction to the truth. أَحَقُّ أَن يُتَّبَعَ- is he more worthy, more qualified, more deserving that he be followed أَمَّن لَّا يَهِدِّىٓ إِلَّآ أَن يُهْدَىٰ- as opposed to the one who's not even in the least bit committed to guidance himself?

The verb here is interesting - يَهِدِّىٓ is a shortened form of يَهْتَدِي, which means to be committed to the truth. So the question is: is the one who guides people to the truth more worthy of being followed, or the one who can't even guide himself, who isn't even committed to guidance? And the only way that blind one can be guided is إِلَّآ أَن يُهْدَىٰ- except that someone else gives him guidance.

In other words, the idol can't even guide itself. It can't have guidance as a description of it except if it is given guidance - meaning it's been physically placed in this direction, raised up, put down, carved, painted. It has no agency of its own.
