commentary:
فَمَآ ءَامَنَ لِمُوسَىٰٓ إِلَّا ذُرِّيَّةٌ مِّن قَوْمِهِۦ عَلَىٰ خَوْفٍ مِّن فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَإِي۟هِمْ أَن يَفْتِنَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ لَعَالٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَإِنَّهُۥ لَمِنَ ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ

Nobody accepted Musa's (AS) call except ذُرِّيَّةٌ- young folks, the offspring, the youth from his nation. Some young people started believing and joining Musa (AS). The phrase عَلَىٰ خَوْفٍ can be interpreted in two equally important ways, and both are legitimate interpretations.

The first meaning uses عَلَىٰ to mean "despite." In this reading, young people from his nation joined him despite their fear of Firawn and his chiefs. The second interpretation explains why so few accepted the call - because the vast majority were paralyzed by their fear of Firawn and his chiefs.

Why were they afraid of Firawn and his chiefs? Because the chiefs were those within the community who would report back to Firawn. When they heard that someone had become Muslim, they would report them immediately. The question arises: when did people start becoming Muslim under Musa's (AS) command in any significant numbers? After the magicians were defeated. But when the magicians were defeated, what announcement did Firawn make? That anybody who believed would be crucified after their limbs were chopped from opposite ends.

So when these young people came to believe, it was despite a very real fear that they would be reported and therefore executed. Many people didn't come to iman because of this fear of execution. Both meanings are therefore covered in the ayah.

The phrase أَن يَفْتِنَهُمْ means "that he will put them to test" - that he will attack them and persecute them. Allah ﷻ then acknowledges Firawn's power: وَإِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ لَعَالٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - no doubt about it, Firawn truly had high authority in the land. His power was no joke. Allah ﷻ Himself acknowledges this reality. وَإِنَّهُۥ لَمِنَ ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ - and he was without doubt from those who go overboard. When he avenged, when he attacked, he didn't care about civilian life.

translation: And Moses said, "O my people, if you have believed in Allah, then rely upon Him, if you should be Muslims
commentary:
وَقَالَ مُوسَىٰ يَـٰقَوْمِ إِن كُنتُمْ ءَامَنتُم بِٱللَّهِ فَعَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلُوٓا۟ إِن كُنتُم مُّسْلِمِينَ

Musa (AS) said, "My nation, if you have truly believed in Allah ﷻ, then only upon Him should you place your trust, if in fact you are Muslims."

translation: So they said, "Upon Allah do we rely. Our Lord, make us not [objects of] trial for the wrongdoing people
commentary:
فَقَالُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ تَوَكَّلْنَا رَبَّنَا لَا تَجْعَلْنَا فِتْنَةً لِّلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

They responded, "Upon Allah ﷻ alone we have placed our trust. Our Master, don't make us a fitnah for the wrongdoing nations."

This is crucial to understand. Doesn't Allah ﷻ test the wrongdoing nations? Yes, He does. He tests them by giving them opportunities to do evil deeds, and they fail the test by committing those evil deeds. The believers are saying: We know You're going to test them, but don't make us the test subjects. In other words, when they commit crimes, don't make us the victims of those crimes. Don't make us the test subjects of their trial.

translation: And save us by Your mercy from the disbelieving people
commentary:
وَنَجِّنَا بِرَحْمَتِكَ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

"And rescue us by Your mercy from the disbelieving nation."

translation: And We inspired to Moses and his brother, "Settle your people in Egypt in houses and make your houses [facing the] qiblah and establish prayer and give good tidings to the believers
commentary:
وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ مُوسَىٰ وَأَخِيهِ أَن تَبَوَّءَا لِقَوْمِكُمَا بِمِصْرَ بُيُوتًا وَٱجْعَلُوا۟ بُيُوتَكُمْ قِبْلَةً وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

Then We revealed to Musa (AS) and his brother: "Find a suitable place to settle for your nation in Egypt. Settle down in homes, and make your homes a qiblah" - meaning they should pray in the direction of the qiblah in those homes. Make your homes the place of prayer, because you can't pray openly yet. It's not public worship yet.

The question arises: what qiblah? Masjid al-Aqsa hasn't even been built yet. This is referring to the original Ka'bah. They were praying towards the Ka'bah. Some scholars have researched Jewish sources and actually confirmed that they prayed towards a city they called the city of Sakha. How convenient - the city of Sakha that Ibrahim (AS) built, but it can't be found anymore. That's quite peculiar.

"And establish the prayer, and give glad tidings to those who truly believe."

translation: And Moses said, "Our Lord, indeed You have given Pharaoh and his establishment splendor and wealth in the worldly life, our Lord, that they may lead [men] astray from Your way. Our Lord, obliterate their wealth and harden their hearts so that they will not believe until they see the painful punishment
commentary:
وَقَالَ مُوسَىٰ رَبَّنَآ إِنَّكَ ءَاتَيْتَ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَأَهُۥ زِينَةً وَأَمْوَٰلًا فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا رَبَّنَا لِيُضِلُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِكَ ۖ رَبَّنَا ٱطْمِسْ عَلَىٰٓ أَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَٱشْدُدْ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ فَلَا يُؤْمِنُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يَرَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ ٱلْأَلِيمَ

Musa (AS) said such a powerful statement: "Our Master, You no doubt gave Firawn and his chiefs beautiful things. You beautified their life, and You gave them lots of wealth in this worldly life. Our Master, You gave them all of those things so they can turn people away from Your path, so they can misguide others from Your path."

Musa (AS) is essentially complaining about an empire that has abundant resources, wealth and beauty. Everyone says if you want to be a successful nation, you should be like them. And Musa (AS) says: You gave them so much so they could become leaders of misguidance. Why did You give them these things? All he's seen is that they've used their power, status and glory - Allah ﷻ Himself acknowledges that Firawn is very high in the land - and all they've used that for is to spread misguidance.

Let me share a reflection about the country we live in. It's an incredible country - the infrastructure, the setup of law and order. It's probably unparalleled in the world in some respects. It leads the world, and the Muslim world has much to learn from it. But the only thing we seem to learn from America is Nike and MTV - the things we shouldn't learn. All the things that are worth learning, we don't learn. We don't import those values.

Muslims sometimes, in their political rage, just hate on a country and all its people. But there are things you'll find in the United States that are hard to find in the Muslim world. The way you'll be treated by others, regardless of color. Try sitting in a restaurant in Pakistan next to someone from a lower class and see the looks exchanged. See how you treat a waiter at a restaurant. Here, it doesn't matter who's serving you - you treat that person with respect because they're an employee. But what happens in the Muslim world?

Some Islamic values, some remnants of them, exist here and are admirable. We should appreciate them. But when a nation is at its glory, people think everything they do must be perfect. So we must Westernize the Muslim world. I agree we should modernize - catch up in technology and infrastructure. But what do we look at first? Values, or sometimes the lack thereof. We want to emulate those.

Our idea of modernizing becomes having Pakistani rock bands, concerts, movies like theirs. We think we should get rid of things that make us primitive. The first thing to go won't be corruption - it will be hijab. Then we need to be a more open society. The first things to come into a Westernized Muslim world will be alcohol and gambling. How about the court system? How about the sanitation system? That's excellent here. How about we take that and leave the gambling and alcohol? But no - nothing of benefit gets taken, everything harmful gets imported.

لِيُضِلُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِكَ - "so they can misguide from Your path." Now Musa (AS) makes a dua against them. We're seeing two angry messengers in this surah. Who was the first? Nuh (AS), quite upset. The second messenger? Musa (AS).

What's remarkable is that the Messenger himself ﷺ is not quoted as being angry. Allah ﷻ tells him to say harsh things, but the Prophet ﷺ doesn't say them of his own accord. Allah ﷻ says: I'm not telling you, you have to say this. But these other messengers reached their limit.

رَبَّنَا ٱطْمِسْ عَلَىٰٓ أَمْوَٰلِهِمْ - "Our Master, obliterate their wealth." The word طَمْس means to obliterate something, crush it, disfigure it, mangle it, break it apart. When someone's face is badly injured and deformed, this word is used. Musa (AS) is asking: disfigure and crush their wealth, leave nothing but a remnant of their money.

وَٱشْدُدْ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ - "and tie up their hearts really tight." This verb of tightening appears in other places in the Quran too. Tie up around their hearts really tight so their hearts are no longer soft and accepting of guidance. The heart has to be soft to accept guidance. Musa (AS) is saying: these people don't deserve guidance anymore. Take their money away, blind their hearts.

فَلَا يُؤْمِنُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يَرَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ ٱلْأَلِيمَ - "so they shouldn't believe until they see the painful punishment." When somebody believes upon seeing the painful punishment, does that belief benefit them? No. At that point, belief is of no consequence, no benefit.

This dua comes towards the end, when Musa (AS) has reached his limit of patience. I personally believe - and Allah ﷻ knows best - that these passages are being revealed to the Prophet ﷺ to show the Quraysh: your Messenger hasn't done this against you. If he had, you would be finished.

translation: [Allah] said, "Your supplication has been answered." So remain on a right course and follow not the way of those who do not know
commentary:
قَالَ قَدْ أُجِيبَت دَّعْوَتُكُمَا فَٱسْتَقِيمَا وَلَا تَتَّبِعَآنِّ سَبِيلَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

Look at the next verse. قَدْ أُجِيبَت دَّعْوَتُكُمَا - "Your prayers, both of you" - who are the two? Musa and Harun (AS). "Your prayer has been responded to. It's done."

فَٱسْتَقِيمَا - "Then stay firm." Don't go back now. Don't say, "Wait, maybe we take it back. That was a little too harsh." Don't go back. Stay firm.

وَلَا تَتَّبِعَآنِّ سَبِيلَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ- "Don't you dare follow" - notice the emphasis, it's doubled - "Don't you dare follow the path of those who refuse, those who don't know." The warning to not follow the path of those without knowledge is given with such force.

translation: And We took the Children of Israel across the sea, and Pharaoh and his soldiers pursued them in tyranny and enmity until, when drowning overtook him, he said, "I believe that there is no deity except that in whom the Children of Israel believe, and I am of the Muslims
commentary:
۞ وَجَـٰوَزْنَا بِبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱلْبَحْرَ فَأَتْبَعَهُمْ فِرْعَوْنُ وَجُنُودُهُۥ بَغْيًا وَعَدْوًا ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَآ أَدْرَكَهُ ٱلْغَرَقُ قَالَ ءَامَنتُ أَنَّهُۥ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِىٓ ءَامَنَتْ بِهِۦ بَنُوٓا۟ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ وَأَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ

We had the Children of Israel cross the body of water, فَأَتْبَعَهُمْ فِرْعَوْنُ وَجُنُودُهُۥ - and Firawn and his armies pursued them closely. بَغْيًا وَعَدْوًا- going beyond the limits, transgressing out of animosity and hate.

The Quran's use of idioms is so powerful. حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَآ أَدْرَكَهُ ٱلْغَرَقُ - "until the time when drowning finally got to him." أَدْرَكَ comes from دَرَكَ. I've mentioned before the contrast between دَرَجَة and دَرَكَة - darajah is when you take steps going up the stairs, darakah is when you take steps going down. So إِدْرَاك in this context is phenomenal. It describes descending immediately or slowly, step by step. The bottom step is drowning itself.

Allah ﷻ is saying that Firawn had been drowning step by step, little by little, ever since the pursuit began. The drowning had already begun, but it finally reached the actual physical drowning. He was coming down gradually, but the final, lowest point he reaches is in the water.

قَالَ ءَامَنتُ - "I believe." But even when he's held belief, what does he say? أَنَّهُۥ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِىٓ ءَامَنَتْ بِهِۦ بَنُوٓا۟ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ - When a Muslim says لَا إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ, how do we finish it? إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ. But it's too arrogant for Firawn. He can't do it. It hurts too much.

"I believe in the same One that the Children of Israel believe in." He can't say the words directly. "I believed in? Yeah, that One. Yeah, sorry." وَأَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ - "and I am from those who submit." Sure, whatever you say.

translation: Now? And you had disobeyed [Him] before and were of the corrupters
commentary:
ءَآلْـَٔـٰنَ وَقَدْ عَصَيْتَ قَبْلُ وَكُنتَ مِنَ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ

ءَآلْـَٔـٰنَ- "Now? Has this come now?" وَقَدْ عَصَيْتَ قَبْلُ- "And you were the one disobeying all this time before." 

Notice قَبْلُ - usually you find مِن قَبْلُ. How would I translate min qablu? "Much before, a long time ago." But قَبْلُ without min? "A second ago. Not long ago, you were disoeying. You were disobeying just a second ago."

وَكُنتَ مِنَ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ - "And you had been from those who cause corruption."

translation: So today We will save you in body that you may be to those who succeed you a sign. And indeed, many among the people, of Our signs, are heedless
commentary:
فَٱلْيَوْمَ نُنَجِّيكَ بِبَدَنِكَ لِتَكُونَ لِمَنْ خَلْفَكَ ءَايَةً ۚ وَإِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا لَغَـٰفِلُونَ

فَٱلْيَوْمَ نُنَجِّيكَ بِبَدَنِكَ- "Then today, We will rescue you with your body."

There are different words in Arabic for body - بَدَن, جَسَد, and others, all found in the Quran. The body that retains blood and the blood hasn't dried up yet - that's badan. A body that's still intact is badan, whether it's dead or alive, as long as it hasn't been destroyed.

لِتَكُونَ لِمَنْ خَلْفَكَ ءَايَةً- "So that you can become a miraculous sign for those who come behind you, for those who come after you."

وَإِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا لَغَـٰفِلُونَ- "And truly there is a huge number of people who are particularly against Our ayat - they are completely heedless."

There's a children's book called "Tell Me About Musa" that I used to have for my kids. It mentions something I've never had the chance to verify, but would like to. Egyptologists reportedly found a mummy of a Firawn that they historically date to the era of Musa (AS). They found signs of marine life on the corpse, and they can't explain why. This was a late 1800s discovery. If that's the case, Allah ﷻ says, "Today We will rescue your body so it can be a miraculous sign for those who come much after you" - khalfaka, those who come behind you.

translation: And We had certainty settled the Children of Israel in an agreeable settlement and provided them with good things. And they did not differ until [after] knowledge had come to them. Indeed, your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that over which they used to differ
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ بَوَّأْنَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ مُبَوَّأَ صِدْقٍ وَرَزَقْنَـٰهُم مِّنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ فَمَا ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْعِلْمُ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِى بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فِيمَا كَانُوا۟ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ

وَلَقَدْ بَوَّأْنَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ مُبَوَّأَ صِدْقٍ- "Then We settled the Children of Israel where they should have been settled, in the settling of truth." The settling was tough, difficult originally, but the advantage was it was based on truth. Now they're living a life of revelation.

وَرَزَقْنَـٰهُم مِّنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ- "Then We provided them from good and pure things."

فَمَا ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْعِلْمُ- "Then they didn't fall into disagreement until knowledge came to them." Until knowledge had come to them, they didn't disagree.

إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِى بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فِيمَا كَانُوا۟ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ- "No doubt about it, your Master will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection in all the things they used to disagree about."

translation: So if you are in doubt, [O Muhammad], about that which We have revealed to you, then ask those who have been reading the Scripture before you. The truth has certainly come to you from your Lord, so never be among the doubters
commentary:
فَإِن كُنتَ فِى شَكٍّ مِّمَّآ أَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ فَسْـَٔلِ ٱلَّذِينَ يَقْرَءُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِن قَبْلِكَ ۚ لَقَدْ جَآءَكَ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ فَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمُمْتَرِينَ

فَإِن كُنتَ فِى شَكٍّ مِّمَّآ أَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ- "Then if you are in any sort of doubt about what We have sent down to you" - 

فَسْـَٔلِ ٱلَّذِينَ يَقْرَءُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِن قَبْلِكَ- "then ask those who have been reading the book for much before you."

Allah ﷻ is saying: people that are really reading the book with sincerity, the people of the book who are reading revelation with sincerity - if they are truly exposed to this revelation, they will know this is the truth. They will testify to it.

لَقَدْ جَآءَكَ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ- "The truth has already come to you from your Master."

فَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمُمْتَرِينَ- "Then don't you become from those who fall into doubt."

I've mentioned before that مُمْتَرِي is someone who doubts a well-established fact that keeps getting attacked. Even though it's a well-established fact, you start doubting it because of the constant, persistent criticisms and attacks against that fact. The Messenger ﷺ is being told: your deen is a well-established fact, but you will be constantly criticized. People will try to poke holes in your deen. Don't let that affect you.

translation: And never be of those who deny the signs of Allah and [thus] be among the losers
commentary:
وَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ فَتَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ

وَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ- "And don't you dare become from those who lie against the miraculous signs of Allah ﷻ."

فَتَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ- "Then you will become from those who will be among the losers."

Remember how this began? In the beginning of the surah, the disbelievers said, "How come you can't change the Quran? How come you can't bring something else?" The Prophet ﷺ responded that he has no authority to do that. Allah ﷻ proves this at the end by telling His Messenger ﷺ: Don't you dare lie against the ayat of Allah ﷻ.

Of course the Messenger ﷺ would never do that. But this is a display of the fact that these aren't his words. Why would he criticize himself? Why would he scold himself and say, "Don't you dare lie against the ayat of Allah ﷻ"? The warnings are coming to the Messenger ﷺ from Allah ﷻ, and this is further validation that he is in fact Allah's Messenger ﷺ.

translation: Indeed, those upon whom the word of your Lord has come into effect will not believe
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ حَقَّتْ عَلَيْهِمْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ

No doubt about it—those upon whom the word of your Master has materialized, the final verdict of your Master has become reality, they will not come to believe. This ayah speaks to a sealed fate. When Allah ﷻ declares His judgment upon a people, their hearts become locked against faith regardless of what evidence comes their way.

translation: Even if every sign should come to them, until they see the painful punishment
commentary:
وَلَوْ جَآءَتْهُمْ كُلُّ ءَايَةٍ حَتَّىٰ يَرَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ ٱلْأَلِيمَ

Even if every single miraculous sign possible were to come to them, it would make no difference. The ayah continues the thought—these people won't believe until they see the painful punishment itself. At that point, belief becomes meaningless because it arrives only when escape is impossible.

translation: Then has there not been a [single] city that believed so its faith benefited it except the people of Jonah? When they believed, We removed from them the punishment of disgrace in worldly life and gave them enjoyment for a time
commentary:
فَلَوْلَا كَانَتْ قَرْيَةٌ ءَامَنَتْ فَنَفَعَهَآ إِيمَـٰنُهَآ إِلَّا قَوْمَ يُونُسَ لَمَّآ ءَامَنُوا۟ كَشَفْنَا عَنْهُمْ عَذَابَ ٱلْخِزْىِ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَمَتَّعْنَـٰهُمْ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ

How come there wasn't a town that believed and benefitted from its faith? Nation after nation has been destroyed throughout history. How come no nation seized the opportunity when their messenger came to them—except the people of Prophet Yunus (AS)?

To understand this exception, we must recognize what happened with the nation of Yunus (AS). He left his people in anger. In some traditions, he warned them they would be destroyed in three days, and then he departed without divine permission. The people had rebelled against him, disbelieved in him, and dismissed his message. When he left, Allah's punishment began to descend upon them.

Normally, when punishment arrives, belief no longer benefits anyone. It makes no difference at that point—you will be destroyed. Just as Pharaoh's declaration of faith while drowning made no difference. But in the case of Yunus's nation, there was something incomplete on the messenger's part, something missing. This worked to the credit of the nation and ended up saving them.

When they saw the punishment coming and made sincere repentance, it was actually removed. They were not destroyed. Allah ﷻ says: when they came to believe, We removed from them the punishment of great humiliation in worldly life, and We provided for them until a given time. They received a fresh lease of existence because of the unique circumstances involving their messenger.

translation: And had your Lord willed, those on earth would have believed - all of them entirely. Then, [O Muhammad], would you compel the people in order that they become believers
commentary:
وَلَوْ شَآءَ رَبُّكَ لَـَٔامَنَ مَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ كُلُّهُمْ جَمِيعًا ۚ أَفَأَنتَ تُكْرِهُ ٱلنَّاسَ حَتَّىٰ يَكُونُوا۟ مُؤْمِنِينَ

Had your Master wanted, everyone on earth would have believed. What happened with the nation of Yunus (AS)—where punishment came, then stopped, and they became believers—could have been Allah's way with all nations. But that is not how He wants people to come to Islam. That exceptional case is not the rule.

Allah ﷻ wants people to come to faith on their own, without coercion. So He asks His Messenger ﷺ: Are you going to force people against their will? Are you going to coerce people until they become believers? No, that will not be your approach. This is not the method Allah ﷻ takes in offering guidance. As He says elsewhere, "There is no compulsion in religion."

translation: And it is not for a soul to believe except by permission of Allah, and He will place defilement upon those who will not use reason
commentary:
وَمَا كَانَ لِنَفْسٍ أَن تُؤْمِنَ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَيَجْعَلُ ٱلرِّجْسَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ

It is not fitting for any person to believe except by Allah's permission. The key word here is permission—إِذْنِ. He places the curse, the punishment of disbelief, upon those who refuse to use their intellect. These are people who refuse to engage their minds, the very means Allah ﷻ has given us to arrive at faith. True believers are described as الَّذِينَ يَعْقِلُونَ (those who use their intellect).

translation: Say, "Observe what is in the heavens and earth." But of no avail will be signs or warners to a people who do not believe
commentary:
قُلِ ٱنظُرُوا۟ مَاذَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَمَا تُغْنِى ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتُ وَٱلنُّذُرُ عَن قَوْمٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ

Tell them: Take a good look at whatever is in the skies and the earth. Notice how the ayah separates الْآيَاتُ (the signs) from النُّذُرُ (the warnings). The ayat here means miracles—no amount of miracles and no amount of warnings will benefit a nation that doesn't want to believe. Once that rejection settles in their hearts, none of these things make any difference.

translation: So do they wait except for like [what occurred in] the days of those who passed on before them? Say, "Then wait; indeed, I am with you among those who wait
commentary:
فَهَلْ يَنتَظِرُونَ إِلَّا مِثْلَ أَيَّامِ ٱلَّذِينَ خَلَوْا۟ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ قُلْ فَٱنتَظِرُوٓا۟ إِنِّى مَعَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْمُنتَظِرِينَ

Are they waiting for anything except the likes of those days that came to those who passed away long before them? Then tell them: Wait, and I am waiting alongside you. We have heard these words before in this surah—this is the second time we're being told إِنِّي مَعَكُم مِّنَ الْمُنتَظِرِينَ (I am waiting alongside you).

translation: Then We will save our messengers and those who have believed. Thus, it is an obligation upon Us that We save the believers
commentary:
ثُمَّ نُنَجِّى رُسُلَنَا وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنَا نُنجِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

Then We rescue Our messengers and those who believe. This is how it is—it is a manifested right upon Us that We rescue the believers. Notice the powerful word حَقًّا—an absolute right mandated upon Us. It is Allah's established pattern throughout history to save the believers.

translation: Say, [O Muhammad], "O people, if you are in doubt as to my religion - then I do not worship those which you worship besides Allah; but I worship Allah, who causes your death. And I have been commanded to be of the believers
commentary:
قُلْ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ إِن كُنتُمْ فِى شَكٍّ مِّن دِينِى فَلَآ أَعْبُدُ ٱلَّذِينَ تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَـٰكِنْ أَعْبُدُ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِى يَتَوَفَّىٰكُمْ ۖ وَأُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

Tell them, O people: If after all of this you are still in doubt about my religion, then know that I will not worship the ones you worship besides Allah ﷻ. Rather, I worship Allah ﷻ alone—the One who will take all of you away in death. I have been commanded to be from those who truly believe.

This is the declaration of بَرَاءَة (absolution). Just as Surah Tawbah contained a social and political declaration of separation from contracts and agreements, by the end of this surah the Messenger ﷺ is told to declare his spiritual and theological separation. After presenting all the evidence, all the arguments, all the stories of past nations, the time comes to say: This is where I stand.

translation: And [commanded], 'Direct your face toward the religion, inclining to truth, and never be of those who associate others with Allah
commentary:
وَأَنْ أَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفًا وَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ

And that you establish your face firmly for the religion as a hanif, and never be from those who associate partners with Allah ﷻ.

An important linguistic consideration helps us understand this ayah. There is أَقَامَ (to establish) and قَامَ (to stand). When Allah ﷻ commands believers regarding prayer, He says قُومُوا لِلصَّلَاةِ (stand for prayer), but He also uses أَقِيمُوا الصَّلَاةَ (establish the prayer). What's the difference?

When you say "I stood," that's complete by itself—it's intransitive. But "I established" requires an object—it's transitive. The believer is told to establish prayer because prayer is like an institution, a building you must erect and maintain. You must care for its foundations, its structure. You must put work into it. That's how you establish something—you make something stand that requires effort and dedication.

So when أَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ (establish your face) is used here, it implies more than just turning toward the religion. It means become fully committed, as though that commitment is an institution you must build inside yourself. Keep your face firm for the religion حَنِيفًا —solely dedicated, unbiased, right down the middle. A حَنِيف is someone without biases, completely devoted. The opposite would be someone who takes sides, who has preferences that pull them away from pure devotion to Allah ﷻ.

translation: And do not invoke besides Allah that which neither benefits you nor harms you, for if you did, then indeed you would be of the wrongdoers
commentary:
وَلَا تَدْعُ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَنفَعُكَ وَلَا يَضُرُّكَ ۖ فَإِن فَعَلْتَ فَإِنَّكَ إِذًا مِّنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

And don't you dare call upon anyone other than Allah ﷻ—anything that doesn't benefit you and doesn't harm you.

When we see the singular form of address, we think of the Messenger ﷺ as the primary addressee, but by extension we are all being addressed. There is an important difference between collective warning and individual warning. If I'm addressing an entire class, that's one thing. But if I single out one student, the impact is far greater. The criticism feels more direct, more personal.

In psychology, this is called the diffusion of responsibility. When everyone is told something collectively, nobody takes individual responsibility. But when one person is specifically told, "Get your act together," they respond. Even if the exact same message was delivered to a group, it wouldn't have the same effect.

Sometimes Allah ﷻ speaks to us individually to give us an urgent sense of personal responsibility. He's talking directly to you—take care of it. This is in addition to the Messenger ﷺ being the primary addressee.

The ayah concludes: If you were to do so—if even you, O Messenger, were to call upon others besides Allah ﷻ—then you would be from those who do wrong.

translation: And if Allah should touch you with adversity, there is no remover of it except Him; and if He intends for you good, then there is no repeller of His bounty. He causes it to reach whom He wills of His servants. And He is the Forgiving, the Merciful
commentary:
وَإِن يَمْسَسْكَ ٱللَّهُ بِضُرٍّ فَلَا كَاشِفَ لَهُۥٓ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ وَإِن يُرِدْكَ بِخَيْرٍ فَلَا رَآدَّ لِفَضْلِهِۦ ۚ يُصِيبُ بِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

Notice a word that keeps appearing throughout this surah: كَاشِفَ (one who removes, uncovers, alleviates). Certain surahs have signature words—particular vocabulary that creates a texture and theme. These words are like colors in a design pattern. The Quran sometimes decorates a surah with specific recurring words, and sometimes with rhyme schemes. When you pay attention, you notice: that word again, and again.

If Allah ﷻ touches you with harm, no one can remove it except He. But look at the contrast: the previous part said "if He touches you with harm," but this part doesn't say "if He touches you with good." Instead it says: if He intends good for you. Once He intends it, it's done. Once He decides upon good for you, it is accomplished.

Then there is no one who can cancel it, take it away, or hold back the blessing He will bestow. He targets with it—He shoots His blessings at—whoever He wants from among His slaves. And no matter how many people try to remove it from them, they will receive it.

And He is الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ (the Exceedingly Forgiving, the Constantly Merciful). Consider why Allah ﷻ mentions these two names specifically in this context. When someone is in difficulty, they may say things or do things that require seeking forgiveness. And when Allah ﷻ removes the difficulty and brings good to them, they should recognize it as mercy upon them. The pairing of these divine names reminds us: seek His forgiveness in hardship, and acknowledge His mercy in relief.

translation: Say, "O mankind, the truth has come to you from your Lord, so whoever is guided is only guided for [the benefit of] his soul, and whoever goes astray only goes astray [in violation] against it. And I am not over you a manager
commentary:
قُلْ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ قَدْ جَآءَكُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ فَمَنِ ٱهْتَدَىٰ فَإِنَّمَا يَهْتَدِى لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن ضَلَّ فَإِنَّمَا يَضِلُّ عَلَيْهَا ۖ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ عَلَيْكُم بِوَكِيلٍ

This is the concluding address of the surah. Say: O people, the truth has come to you from your Master.

The surah began with the grandeur of the book—why is it so strange for you to accept this messenger? Why is it so weird for you to accept this book? And the surah ends in a similar place: the book has come to you. Whoever commits themselves to guidance and holds onto it will only benefit themselves. Whoever is misled is only misguided against their own soul.

I am not in any way the caretaker of your matters. I am not going to dispose of your affairs for you. The Prophet ﷺ cannot force outcomes. He cannot manage people's hearts. He can only deliver the message.

translation: And follow what is revealed to you, [O Muhammad], and be patient until Allah will judge. And He is the best of judges
commentary:
وَٱتَّبِعْ مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَيْكَ وَٱصْبِرْ حَتَّىٰ يَحْكُمَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلْحَـٰكِمِينَ

Continue to follow what is being revealed to you, and continue to be patient until Allah ﷻ gives His verdict.

The Prophet ﷺ is being told: In Makkah, you don't see results yet. But keep doing what you're doing. Keep following the instructions and the ayat being given to you. Keep reciting them to the people. Keep calling them to this message. Until further notice from Me, you will stay on this path. Those further instructions—how they developed into eventual victory—we have gotten glimpses of. But here we're taking a step back to the moment before victory was established. Allah ﷻ told His Messenger ﷺ: Be patient and work with what you have right now. Stay focused on the task at hand.

And He is the best of all those who rule.

---

## Reflections on Social Change and the Prophetic Model

As this surah concludes, it's worth reflecting on something deeply relevant to our times: the prophetic blueprint for bringing about change in society. There are real advantages in studying the sirah of the Prophet ﷺ as a model for social transformation. The sirah itself is one giant sunnah of change—not just small individual deeds, but a macro-sunnah showing how to bring goodness to an entire society.

However, there's a danger we must avoid. When we look back at the sirah, we have the benefit of hindsight. We see events in sequence: this happened, then this happened, which led to this, which resulted in this. This creates a temptation to view the sirah as a formula. Some Islamic movements have fallen into this trap, thinking: "These events happened in this order during the Prophet's time, so if we replicate them, we'll get the same results."

The problem is that certain events in the sirah cannot be put into a formula. The hijrah (migration) was miraculous. The way it unfolded, the help of Allah ﷻ that came—you cannot say, "First we'll make dua, things will get difficult, then Allah ﷻ will blind our enemies' eyes and we'll escape." You cannot turn the miracles of Allah ﷻ into a reproducible formula.

It's crucial for Muslims not to become obsessed with stages and timelines—what happens next, what comes after that. Part of the power in how the Companions (RA) were given change is that they were not told what was coming next. They were simply told: اصْبِرْ حَتَّىٰ يَحْكُمَ اللَّهُ (be patient until Allah judges). They weren't informed, "In a couple of years there will be hijrah, Madinah will be established, you'll have a small army, battles will take place, and things will work out—don't worry, I have a whole plan." No, that wasn't how it worked.

The early Makkan struggle of the Prophet ﷺ resembles the struggles of all messengers before him. That's why their stories were brought as examples. The strategy was clear: keep the believers close, remain patient, and don't ask when or how things will change. Just stay focused on what's in front of you.

In management terms, there's something called "analysis paralysis"—you keep analyzing and analyzing what might happen, what could happen, until you're paralyzed and do nothing. Some Muslims fall into this trap, becoming obsessed with signs of the End Times, hadith about the Mahdi, armies in Syria, the Dajjal. These matters are real—we believe in them, they're from the words of the Prophet ﷺ and we don't deny them. But when you become a conspiracy theorist obsessed with these topics, what happens? You stop doing anything productive.

We have clear tasks in front of us. We shouldn't overcomplicate them. We shouldn't get lost discussing details that aren't relevant to us right now. But this does not mean we don't believe our religion will ultimately prevail. This does not mean we don't believe Islam will one day have complete victory. We absolutely believe that. Every Muslim wishes to live under the rule of Allah's law. That's not something to be ashamed of or deny.

If someone asks, "Isn't that politically incorrect?" consider this: Christians pray "Thy kingdom come, Thy will be done on Earth as it is in Heaven." I say Amen to that kingdom coming. We have this hope in common. But to sit around discussing how the Islamic economic system will work or what the governance structure will be—what good does that do when you can't even get basic Islamic institutions in your community to function properly?

It's like two homeless people sitting in a cardboard box in New York City debating what color Ferrari they'll drive. Deal with what's in front of you first. Focusing on immediate priorities is not a denial of what's coming—it's proper prioritization. This is what the Messenger ﷺ was taught: Islam will prevail, but how it unfolds wasn't revealed to him yet. The instruction was: focus on first things first.

If we are sincere to this book and do our job, Allah ﷻ will open those doors. We don't know how. We can't create a formula for it. It will happen the way Allah ﷻ decrees. We must do what's ahead of us, what's directly in front of us.

The biggest institution the Prophet ﷺ built was the individual believer. If you study the hadith and examine their "flavor" from different periods, you'll notice something striking. In Madinah, after the community was established, many hadith emphasize the rewards of good deeds—the virtue of waking up for Fajr, the reward of specific actions, family matters. There's much encouragement for believers to become individually strong through righteous deeds.

But in Makkah, the flavor was different. Muslims were still individuals who could only meet secretly. They couldn't congregate openly. So the focus was different—on fundamental beliefs, on patience under persecution, on holding fast to faith itself. When the Muslims became an established community in Madinah, even the nature of prophetic advice shifted to match their new circumstances.

It's the job of our scholars to guide us in understanding these priorities. What are our priorities as individuals? As families? As communities? As people living in our particular cities and countries? What milestones and goals should we be working toward in dawah, in education, in serving others?

The alternative—becoming overindulged in discussions about the End Times, the coming of the Mahdi, the return of Prophet Isa (AS), while doing nothing practical—misses the entire point. The Prophet ﷺ taught us that if Judgment Day comes and a believer is planting a seed, he should finish planting it. Not drop everything and say, "I told you it was coming!" No—focus on what's in front of you. Complete the task at hand.

That's what we must become, insha'Allah. May Allah ﷻ make us committed to the sunnah of His Messenger ﷺ and to the great legacy of this Ummah. May He help us understand the Quran in its true spirit and apply it in our lives.
